AI.conclusion: Directly supervising visual tokens improves multimodal alignment and fine-grained visual understanding, making DV-SFT a practical and efficient approach for MLLMs.

title: Distribution-Aware Conformal Prediction: A Framework for generating efficient prediction intervals for time series
comment: submitted to Journal of Machine Learning Research (JMLR)
summary: We present Distribution-aware Conformal Prediction (DCP), a unified framework integrating probabilistic predictors like Monte Carlo dropout, deep ensembles, and quantile regression with score-agnostic conformal calibration to produce valid and efficient prediction intervals. Leveraging a numerical inversion approach to construct interval bounds, DCP accommodates arbitrary combinations of distribution generating predictors and nonconformity scores. Benchmark analysis on synthetic and real-world time series data demonstrate DCP's ability to adaptively calibrate prediction intervals under varying uncertainty regimes. Crucially, DCP's modular design facilitates plug-and-play experimentation with different predictor-score pairings, quantitatively supported by a newly introduced modified Winkler score that balances validity and efficiency by explicitly penalizing undercoverage. While DCP generalizes and extends existing approaches like Conformalized Quantile Regression and Conformalized Monte Carlo, its modular design allows further extensions, setting a foundation for advancing uncertainty quantification in dynamic environments and high-risk applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Distribution-aware Conformal Prediction (DCP), a flexible framework for creating valid and efficient prediction intervals by integrating probabilistic predictors with conformal calibration.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to enhance uncertainty quantification by combining probabilistic predictors with a unified and modular framework that ensures accurate and adaptive prediction intervals across various uncertainty scenarios.
AI.method: The authors use a numerical inversion approach to construct interval bounds, making the framework accommodate any combination of distribution-based predictors and scores. A modified Winkler score is used to balance validity and efficiency by penalizing undercoverage.
AI.result: DCP demonstrates robust performance in adaptively calibrating prediction intervals on synthetic and real-world time series data. It generalizes and improves existing methods while providing a customizable modular framework.
AI.conclusion: DCP offers a generalized and extendable approach to uncertainty quantification, creating a foundation for further advancements in dynamic and high-stakes applications.

title: Gumbel Machine: Counterfactual Student Writing Generation via Gumbel Noise Steering
summary: An effective method of teaching across disciplines is to provide examples of high-quality work. However, an example may be significantly different from a student's current work, making it challenging for them to emulate. An ideal learning demonstration is a counterfactual version of the student work, an improved version that is still similar to their own. Existing automated approaches for counterfactual text generation using Large Language Models (LLMs) result in domain-specific systems that are difficult to translate into practical applications. We present the Gumbel Machine, a flexible, modular approach to generating counterfactuals that leverages LLM instruction-following capabilities while encouraging similarity to a reference factual text. Central to our approach is a novel, controlled decoding algorithm, $β$-Hindsight control, which uses latent randomness as a tunable similarity control mechanism during counterfactual generation. Experiments on datasets of student writing, scored on various criteria, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach at generating counterfactuals both rubric-consistent and similar to a reference.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Gumbel Machine, a method that uses large language models and a controlled decoding algorithm ($β$-Hindsight control) to generate improved versions of student work, balancing quality improvement and similarity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to assist teaching by generating improved, yet similar-to-original, examples of student work to aid learning when examples of high-quality work differ significantly from students' attempts.
AI.method: The Gumbel Machine uses a modular approach to generate counterfactuals by combining the instruction-following capabilities of large language models with a novel algorithm ($β$-Hindsight control) that enables controlled similarity during text generation.
AI.result: The approach demonstrated effective generation of counterfactuals on student writing datasets, maintaining both rubric consistency and similarity to the original reference text.
AI.conclusion: This method presents a practical, domain-flexible way to generate counterfactual versions of student work, potentially improving the teaching process across disciplines.

title: AlbanianLLMSafety: A Safety Evaluation Dataset for Large Language Models in Albanian
comment: Accepted at SIGUL2026 Workshop co-located with LREC2026
summary: Safety evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) has largely focused on high-resource languages, leaving low-resource languages critically underserved. We present AlbanianLLMSafety, the first publicly available safety evaluation dataset for LLMs in Albanian, a linguistically distinct low-resource language with approximately 7.5 million speakers across Albania, Kosovo, North Macedonia, and the diaspora. The dataset contains 2,951 prompts spanning 11 safety categories, including self-harm, violence, racist content, child exploitation, and radicalization, with an average of 268 prompts per category. Each prompt is provided in Albanian with an English reference translation and a detailed category label. This resource addresses a significant gap in safety evaluation infrastruc-ture for low-resource languages and provides an essential benchmark for developing safer, more inclusive LLMs. The dataset will be provided upon request to support safety evaluation, fine-tuning, red-teaming, and guardrail development for Albanian-speaking communities.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces AlbanianLLMSafety, the first safety evaluation dataset specifically for Albanian, a low-resource language, containing 2,951 prompts across 11 safety categories.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of safety evaluation resources for low-resource languages, particularly Albanian, to foster safer and more inclusive LLMs.
AI.method: Developed a dataset with 2,951 Albanian prompts, each labeled by safety categories, along with English translations, enabling thorough safety evaluations.
AI.result: Provided a comprehensive safety evaluation dataset for Albanian, covering diverse and critical safety categories, designed to fill a gap in low-resource language evaluation.
AI.conclusion: This dataset serves as a benchmark for improving LLM safety and inclusivity in Albanian-speaking communities, promoting the development of ethical and responsible AI models.

title: Respecting Modality Gap in Post-hoc Out-of-distribution Detection with Pre-trained Vision-Language Models
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection has emerged as a popular technique to enhance the reliability of machine learning models by identifying unexpected inputs from unknown classes. Recent progress in pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) has enabled zero-shot OOD detection without access to in-distribution (ID) training data; in this setting, existing methods commonly treat text embeddings of class names as class prototypes. In this paper, we challenge the widely adopted text-as-prototype paradigm by theoretically showing that off-the-shelf textual prototypes are generally misaligned with the optimal visual prototypes, yielding an intrinsic modality gap that cannot be eliminated by prompt engineering alone. To mitigate this gap under the post-hoc constraint, this paper presents an online pseudo-supervised framework that directly learns class prototypes in the visual feature space using unlabeled test-time data streams and soft predictions from the pre-trained VLMs. We provide theoretical guarantees for the convergence of the online optimization procedure. Extensive experiments empirically demonstrate that our method achieves a new state of the art across a variety of OOD detection setups.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel approach for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection by directly learning visual prototypes instead of using text embeddings, challenging the existing methods in pre-trained vision-language models.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of the existing text-as-prototype paradigm in OOD detection, specifically the modality gap between textual and visual representations in pre-trained vision-language models.
AI.method: The paper introduces an online pseudo-supervised framework to learn class prototypes in the visual feature space. It employs soft predictions from pre-trained models and test-time data streams for optimization, alongside theoretical guarantees for the framework's convergence.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms existing approaches and achieves state-of-the-art results in various OOD detection setups, as demonstrated through extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Directly learning visual prototypes mitigates the modality gap and enhances the zero-shot OOD detection capabilities of vision-language models.

title: Separate Aggregation of Split Network for Personalized Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, but its performance can degrade substantially under heterogeneous client data distributions. A single global model often cannot satisfy diverse client requirements, so personalized federated learning has therefore been explored to improve client specific performance while preserving global generalization. Existing PFL methods often face a fundamental tradeoff in which stronger global sharing can undermine local specialization, whereas stronger local adaptation can lead to overfitting under limited data, label imbalance, and missing class scenarios. In this work, we propose PGFedSplit, a personalized federated learning framework that improves both personalization and global generalization under severe client heterogeneity. PGFedSplit adopts a split architecture and performs adaptive aggregation scheduling tailored to the roles of different model components, enabling stable knowledge sharing while maintaining client specific adaptation. Each client further leverages a mixture of locally extracted representations and synthetic representations generated from server side Gaussian statistics, improving robustness under label imbalance and missing class conditions. Extensive experiments on Fashion MNIST, CIFAR 10, CIFAR 100, and Tiny ImageNet demonstrate consistent improvements over state of the art PFL methods, with stable convergence and superior personalization in highly heterogeneous settings.

AI.tldr: PGFedSplit is a personalized federated learning framework addressing client data heterogeneity by improving personalization and global generalization. It uses split architectures and adaptive aggregation for balanced model development, combined with synthetic and local representations for improved robustness.
AI.motivation: The goal is to tackle the performance degradation problem in federated learning caused by heterogeneous client data distributions while addressing the tradeoff between global sharing and local specialization.
AI.method: PGFedSplit employs a split model architecture with adaptive aggregation scheduling to balance knowledge sharing and client-specific adaptation. It incorporates synthetic representation generation using server-side Gaussian statistics to counter label imbalance and missing class scenarios.
AI.result: Tests on datasets like Fashion MNIST, CIFAR 10, CIFAR 100, and Tiny ImageNet show PGFedSplit achieves enhanced stability, convergence, and performance. It outperformed existing personalized federated learning methods in diverse and highly heterogeneous conditions.
AI.conclusion: PGFedSplit effectively tackles challenges in federated learning under severe data heterogeneity by striking a balance between personalization and global generalization. Its design ensures robustness and improved client-specific results in challenging settings.

title: SIA: Self Improving AI with Harness & Weight Updates
summary: Humans are the bottleneck in building and improving AI. Both the models and the agents that wrap them are written, tuned, and corrected by people. The long-horizon goal of an AI that can figure out how to improve itself remains open. Two largely disjoint research lines attack this bottleneck. The harness-update school has a meta-agent rewrite the scaffold of a task-specific agent (its tools, prompts, retry logic, and search procedure) while the model weights are held fixed. The test-time training school uses hand-written RL pipelines to update the model's own weights on task feedback while the harness is held fixed. These two silos operate in isolation. We propose SIA, a self-improving loop in which a language-model agent (the Feedback-Agent) updates both the harness and the weights of a task-specific agent. We evaluate across three contrasting domains: Chinese legal charge classification, low-level GPU kernel optimisation, and single-cell RNA denoising. Combining both levers outperforms scaffold iteration alone on all three benchmarks. The gains are 56.6% on LawBench, 91.9% runtime reduction on GPU kernels, and 502% on denoising over the initial baseline. Harness updates make the model agentic, shaping how it searches and acts, while weight updates build the domain intuition that no prompt or scaffold can instil.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SIA, a framework where an AI self-improves by updating both task-specific scaffolds and model weights, showing significant performance gains across varied tasks.
AI.motivation: Humans are a bottleneck in AI systems, which rely on manual intervention for tuning and improvement. The authors aim to develop an autonomous approach where AI can self-improve using feedback.
AI.method: The proposed SIA loop involves a language-model-based Feedback-Agent that modifies both the scaffolding (tools, prompts, and retry logic) and the model weights of a task-specific agent, combining the strengths of two isolated research paradigms.
AI.result: SIA achieves significant performance improvement on three domains: Chinese legal classification (56.6% improvement), GPU kernel optimization (91.9% runtime reduction), and single-cell RNA denoising (502% efficiency gain).
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that combining harness updates (for agentic behavior) and weight updates (for domain-specific intuition) leads to substantial improvements, bridging gaps in existing methodologies.

title: JuICE: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-Judge in Identifying Cultural Errors
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed to users around the world, they are integrated into everyday tasks across diverse cultural contexts, from drafting personal communications to brainstorming creative ideas. These tasks are inherently cultural: they require contextual appropriateness, symbolic resonance, and tacit cultural expectations that native speakers draw on instinctively, meaning that a response can be factually plausible yet unmistakably wrong to a local reader. Existing cultural benchmarks have treated culture as a flat set of facts via fact verification or norm entailment methods, and have adopted LLM-as-a-Judge without examining whether they can capture such thick cultural errors. To address this gap, we present JuICE (Benchmark for LLM-Judge in Identifying Cultural Errors), a multilingual dataset of 7,470 span-level annotations of cultural and linguistic errors in long-form LLM responses. It covers 1,050 query-response pairs from four countries (the United States, South Korea, Indonesia, and Bangladesh), in both English and their countries' main languages. Using JuICE, we find that even the strongest LLM-judge achieves only an F1 of 0.52 in the erroneous span detection task. Furthermore, LLM-judges consistently miss thick cultural errors that local residents readily identify. Our findings suggest that robust cultural evaluation must move beyond surface-level detection toward frameworks that account for the depth and situatedness of cultural meaning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces JuICE, a benchmark dataset for evaluating cultural and linguistic error detection by large language models (LLMs), revealing current LLMs struggle with recognizing deep cultural nuances.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become widely used in diverse cultural contexts, there's a need to ensure their responses fit nuanced cultural expectations rather than just surface-level correctness.
AI.method: The authors developed JuICE, a multilingual dataset with 7,470 annotations of cultural and linguistic errors in LLM responses from four countries, spanning both English and local languages.
AI.result: The best-performing LLM-judge achieved an F1 score of only 0.52 and failed to consistently detect 'thick cultural errors' that are easily identified by local humans.
AI.conclusion: Robust LLM deployment in culturally diverse scenarios requires deeper evaluation frameworks that capture complex, context-specific cultural meanings.

title: Memory-Distilled Selection for Noise-Robust Anomaly Detection
comment: Accepted by ICML2026. The code is available at https://github.com/SirojbekSafarov/MeDS
summary: Anomaly detection (AD) under data contamination is critical for deploying unsupervised defect detection in industrial environments, where curating perfectly clean training sets is impractical. However, existing methods are sensitive to contamination, suffering significant performance degradation as the noise ratio increases. In this paper, we propose Memory-Distilled Selection (MeDS), a training algorithm based on data selection. MeDS constructs an ensemble of partial memories via random subsampling, where the resulting sparsity acts as a low-pass filter that captures nominal patterns across a wide range of noise ratios, enabling coarse-level identification of contaminated samples. The aggregated distances to the bootstrapped memories are then distilled into a reconstruction score network, which is subsequently fine-tuned on clean data filtered using scores from the distilled model, enabling fine-grained localization of anomalies. MeDS is robust across a wide range of noise ratios without requiring noise-ratio-specific hyperparameter tuning, achieving 99.16\% image-level AUROC on MVTecAD at a 40\% noise ratio, and attaining state-of-the-art performance on both VisA and Real-IAD under noisy settings. We thoroughly verify the efficacy of MeDS on industrial AD benchmarks under noisy data scenarios, accompanied by in-depth empirical analyses.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an unsupervised anomaly detection algorithm called MeDS, designed to perform under noisy data conditions.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of deploying robust anomaly detection in industrial settings where clean training data is impractical.
AI.method: MeDS uses random subsampling (to create sparse partial memories) for coarse identification of noisy samples and distills aggregated distances into a model for fine-tuned anomaly localization.
AI.result: MeDS achieves state-of-the-art AD performance, including 99.16% AUROC on MVTecAD at a 40% noise ratio, showcasing robustness to noise.
AI.conclusion: MeDS demonstrates effectiveness in noisy industrial anomaly detection scenarios without the need for noise-specific tuning.

title: Focal Reward: Balanced Reinforcement Learning under Rubric-Based Rewards
summary: The open-ended generation in LLMs usually requires multi-dimensional rubrics to adequately assess quality and guide the improvement of reinforcement learning. However, a critical dilemma inherent in this training paradigm is the imbalanced reward polarization along different rubric dimensions. Under this bottleneck, even if LLMs achieve relatively high rewards after training, they may still exhibit severe deficiencies in certain dimensions, leading to a direct deterioration in user experience. To address this problem, we propose Focal Reward, a novel objective to automatically balance the training of reinforcement learning under rubric-based rewards. Specifically, we first leverage an inverse reward projection mechanism to estimate the saturation degree of each criterion in the rubric, which forms the basis to calibrate the reward direction. Then, the final objective is designed with an automatically reweighting coefficient for each criterion to achieve the fine-grained balancing. Extensive experiments across three model scales and six benchmarks demonstrate that our Focal Reward method outperforms the strongest static aggregation baseline in all 18 model-benchmark comparisons. Rollout, mechanism, and ablation analyses further show that these gains arise from online, saturation-aware reallocation toward rubrics that still have room for improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces 'Focal Reward,' a method to automatically balance reinforcement learning objectives in large language models (LLMs) by addressing reward imbalances across different quality dimensions.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the observation that reinforcement learning in LLMs, guided by rubric-based rewards, often leads to imbalanced improvements across dimensions, deteriorating user experience despite high overall rewards.
AI.method: The proposed approach employs an inverse reward projection to estimate saturation levels for each criterion and dynamically adjusts reward weights to achieve balanced training under rubric-based objectives.
AI.result: Experiments across various model scales and benchmarks show that Focal Reward consistently outperforms static aggregation baselines, demonstrating improved balancing and overall model performance.
AI.conclusion: Focal Reward addresses the imbalanced reward polarization issue in LLMs, offering a saturation-aware and fine-tuned solution that enhances the training and performance of reinforcement learning systems.

title: Modeling Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax: A Standalone Framework for Measurement, Simulation, and Dashboarding
summary: Agentic AI systems combine probabilistic reasoning with delegated action through tools, context, memory, orchestration, and external workflow integration. This note develops a formal and managerially usable model that distinguishes Agentic Technical Debt from Stochastic Tax. Agentic Technical Debt is a stock of accumulated design and governance liability. Stochastic Tax is a recurring flow of operating burden that arises when stochastic agents are used in business workflows. The two constructs are related, but they are not the same: debt can amplify the tax, while the tax can remain positive even when debt is minimized. The note starts from a compact dashboard expression, expands it into a fuller structural model, defines all variables and parameters, shows how each cost category can be estimated from operational data, and illustrates the framework with an accounts-payable simulation and companion spreadsheet.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the concepts of 'Agentic Technical Debt' and 'Stochastic Tax' for AI systems, and provides a model to estimate and understand these costs.
AI.motivation: To help managers and businesses better understand and manage the costs and liabilities associated with integrating stochastic AI agents into workflows.
AI.method: The authors propose a formalized structural model for understanding and quantifying Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax, using operational data and illustrative simulations.
AI.result: The model links operational costs to these concepts and is demonstrated with an accounts-payable simulation and a spreadsheet tool.
AI.conclusion: The work enables clearer distinctions and estimations of the costs associated with agentic AI systems, helping organizations manage these challenges more effectively.

title: Tournament-GRPO: Group-Wise Tournament Rewards for Reinforcement Learning in Open-Ended Long-Form Generation
summary: Reinforcement learning in open-ended long-form generation is challenging because reliable reference answers and automatic metrics are often unavailable. Existing rubric-based methods typically rely on pointwise LLM-as-a-judge scoring, but absolute scores are difficult to calibrate across complex responses, may provide weak discrimination among same-query rollouts, and can become saturated during optimization. We propose Tournament-GRPO, a group-wise reward framework that converts rubric-guided LLM judgments into relative rewards through repeated multi-round tournaments among same-query rollouts. Tournament-GRPO compares candidates within groups, accumulates tournament outcomes, and normalizes them into group-wise rewards for GRPO training. Experiments on Deep Research Bench show that Tournament-GRPO consistently outperforms existing reward-design baselines, achieving a 4.52-point overall-score improvement over the strongest baseline. Further analyses show that tournament rewards provide a favorable effectiveness--efficiency trade-off and that tournament design affects training dynamics. These results suggest that rubric-guided tournament comparison provides an effective reward signal for reinforcement learning in open-ended long-form generation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Tournament-GRPO, a new reinforcement learning method for open-ended text generation that uses tournaments to compare outputs relatively instead of using absolute scoring.
AI.motivation: Open-ended long-form text generation with reinforcement learning is difficult due to the lack of reliable reference answers and the challenges in using existing scoring methods, which struggle with calibration, weak discrimination, and optimization saturation.
AI.method: The authors developed Tournament-GRPO, a reward framework that organizes multiple tournament rounds among text outputs for the same query, scores them relatively, and uses these group-based scores for GRPO training.
AI.result: Tournament-GRPO surpassed existing baselines on the Deep Research Bench, showing a 4.52-point improvement in overall performance and demonstrating an advantageous effectiveness--efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The proposed rubric-guided tournament comparison offers a robust reward signal for reinforcement learning, improving text generation quality in the absence of absolute scoring standards.

title: DynFrame: Adaptive Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Framework with Dynamic Frame Augmentation for Complex Video Understanding
summary: Recent video multimodal large language models (MLLMs) increasingly couple step-by-step reasoning with on-demand visual evidence retrieval, allowing models to revisit relevant video segments during inference. However, two structural gaps remain in existing thinking-with-video systems. (i) Sampling density is not a learnable decision: existing methods may let the model decide where to look, but the per-window frame rate is largely fixed. As a result, fine-grained evidence is often recovered through repeated retrieval calls, which increases inference context length and training difficulty. (ii) Retrieval and answer generation are usually optimized with a single trajectory-level advantage, so the "where to look" tokens and the "how to answer" tokens receive the same credit even when one is correct and the other is not. To address these gaps, we present DynFrame, a framework that emits the temporal window and the sampling density as native tokens within a single autoregressive pass. This learnable span-density retrieval enables acquiring multi-granularity evidence with a single retrieval step. Based on the above tokenized retrieval interface, we further introduce Segment-Decoupled GRPO (SD-GRPO), which splits each rollout at the retrieval boundary and assigns role-specific token-level advantages, separately crediting the sampling decision and the answer. Trained on the curated DM-CoT-74k and DM-RL-45k, DynFrame-4B is competitive with strong 7B-8B baselines across six benchmarks (NExT-GQA, Charades-STA, ActivityNet-MR, Video-MME, MLVU, LVBench), and DynFrame-8B sets new state-of-the-art on most metrics. Code is available at https://github.com/zhangguanghao523/DynFrame.

AI.tldr: DynFrame enhances video multimodal language models (MLLM) by introducing learnable sampling density and tokenized retrieval interfaces for more efficient and accurate reasoning with video evidence.
AI.motivation: To address structural limitations in video MLLMs where sampling density decisions are not learnable and retrieval-answer optimization lacks specificity, leading to inefficiencies in retrieval and reasoning.
AI.method: The DynFrame framework uses a tokenized retrieval interface that includes learnable temporal window and sampling density tokens, and employs Segment-Decoupled GRPO (SD-GRPO) for separate credit assignment to retrieval and answer tokens.
AI.result: DynFrame-4B performs competitively against larger 7B-8B baselines on six benchmarks, while DynFrame-8B achieves state-of-the-art results on most metrics.
AI.conclusion: DynFrame provides a more efficient and accurate approach for integrating coarse and fine-grained evidence retrieval in video MLLMs through learnable sampling and retrieval-answer decoupling.

title: On the Error-Correcting Effects of Stochasticity in Discrete Diffusion
summary: Discrete diffusion models achieve strong performance in text and image generation, but their inference remains slow and must inherently balance sampling efficiency and sample quality. In this work, we present a systematic study of how the \emph{degree of stochasticity} in Markov transitions governs the sampling tradeoff. We show that highly deterministic transitions converge rapidly but suffer from error accumulation, while more stochastic transitions converge more slowly yet can achieve higher final sample quality. Using an information-theoretic analysis, we identify the underlying mechanism as an error-correcting effect induced by \emph{redundant transitions} that symmetrically exchange mass between states, and show that these transitions can provably contract sampling errors. Motivated by this analysis, we propose \emph{Discrete Churn and Restart Sampling} (DCRS), a novel inference algorithm that injects controlled stochasticity by alternating between forward and reverse diffusion processes. Experiments on synthetic datasets and large-scale benchmarks show that DCRS improves the speed-quality tradeoff in the low number of function evaluations regime. On image datasets, DCRS achieves up to a $10\times$ reduction in sampling steps compared to standard samplers while maintaining competitive sample quality, whereas on language benchmarks, we observe more nuanced behavior depending on the corruption process and sampling procedure.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the balance between sampling efficiency and sample quality in discrete diffusion models and proposes a new mechanism, DCRS, that improves the tradeoff through controlled stochasticity during inference.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiencies in inference for discrete diffusion models caused by balancing sampling speed and sample quality, which affects practical applications in text and image generation.
AI.method: The authors perform an in-depth information-theoretic analysis to understand the impact of stochasticity in Markov transitions and introduce the DCRS algorithm, which alternates controlled forward and reverse diffusion processes to inject stochasticity.
AI.result: Empirical results show that DCRS significantly reduces the number of inference steps (up to 10× fewer on image datasets) while maintaining competitive sample quality. Results vary in tasks like language generation, depending on the specific corruption process and sampling procedure.
AI.conclusion: DCRS demonstrates strong potential in improving the speed-quality tradeoff for discrete diffusion models, especially in image generation tasks, and offers new insights for further exploration in related domains.

title: Maat: The Agentic Legal Research Assistant for Competition Protection
summary: Competition law experts conducting legal research must review extensive volumes of cases, decisions, and judicial reports to identify precedents and assess key elements in competition and merger cases. Although general research assistants such as Claude and ChatGPT and legal assistants such as SaulLM-7B and LegalGPT are increasingly used to assist legal research, they remain inadequate for competition law analysis: they lack specialized domain expertise, provide insufficient official citations, or hallucinate competition law cases. We propose Maat, a ReAct agent that orchestrates tools corresponding to different tasks of the research process. Designed iteratively with competition law experts, Maat grounds cases and findings in official sources using RAG for reliability, provides rich in-line citations, falls back to web search when database coverage is insufficient, and prompts the user for clarification when queries are ambiguous. Maat significantly outperforms all baseline assistants on case-specific tasks and performs within range of the top baseline on theoretical question tasks. The dataset used is available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Maat, a specialized ReAct agent geared for competition law research, designed to overcome limitations like lack of domain expertise and poor citation grounding found in existing legal AI tools.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address shortcomings in current legal and general research assistants like ChatGPT, SaulLM-7B, and LegalGPT, which are inadequate for competition law analysis due to issues such as lack of specialized knowledge, insufficient citation reliability, and hallucination of cases.
AI.method: Maat employs a ReAct agent that integrates tools for various tasks in competition law research. It incorporates Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for reliable citation grounding, web search fallback for incomplete databases, and user clarification prompts for ambiguous queries. It was designed iteratively with domain experts.
AI.result: Maat outperforms all baselines on case-specific tasks and achieves comparable performance with the leading baseline on theoretical questions. The dataset utilized for the evaluation is publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Maat effectively addresses the identified gaps in existing legal research assistants by ensuring reliability, comprehensive citation support, and specialized domain expertise for competition law analysis.

title: Recon: Reconstruction-Guided Reasoning Synthesis for User Modeling
summary: User modeling aims to use language models (LMs) to mimic an individual's behavior from a corpus of past context-action pairs (e.g., conversation turns), enabling the simulation of users in settings like behavioral science, human-AI collaboration, and market research. Recent approaches augment these corpora with synthesized reasoning traces, typically generated by conditioning on both context and action. However, such conditioning constitutes post-hoc rationalization rather than reasoning: the trace is guaranteed to justify the action, but may not encode the underlying latent causal decision paths. We propose Recon, which uses action reconstruction to score reasoning traces by their predictive power: given a context and candidate reasoning, a reconstruction model predicts the action, and reconstruction fidelity determines reasoning quality. Across four domains, Recon achieves a 54.7% win rate over Backward Synthesis, a standard post-hoc rationalization baseline. Further, we find that training a reasoning synthesis model with rewards derived from Recon improves downstream user modeling performance, achieving a win rate of up to 70.0% over baselines. We further show that Recon-synthesized reasoning transfers across models, and improves user modeling beyond the reconstruction model. Our work demonstrates that post-hoc rationalization is insufficient for reasoning synthesis, and that useful and interpretable reasoning should naturally elicit the action from the context.

AI.tldr: User modeling often uses reasoning traces to simulate behavior, but traces from methods like Backward Synthesis can lack genuine causal pathways. Recon introduces action reconstruction to evaluate reasoning trace quality, achieving better results in four domains and improving user modeling by synthesizing more effective reasoning.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of effectively modeling user behavior by evaluating reasoning traces. Current methods often rely on post-hoc rationalization, which, while justifying user actions, may fail to capture true causal decision-making pathways.
AI.method: The proposed method, Recon, scores reasoning traces by measuring their predictive power via action reconstruction. The model predicts actions from a given context and reasoning trace, and the fidelity of this reconstruction evaluates reasoning quality. Recon also rewards reasoning synthesis models to enhance user modeling.
AI.result: Recon achieves a 54.7% win rate over the Backward Synthesis baseline and helps improve downstream user modeling performance to reach a win rate of up to 70.0% when trained with Recon-derived rewards. The effectiveness of Recon-synthesized reasoning extends across models and improves performance beyond the reconstruction model itself.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc rationalization is inadequate for reasoning synthesis in user modeling. High-quality reasoning should elicit actions naturally from the given context. Recon bridges this gap by leveraging action reconstruction, delivering superior performance and transferability across multiple domains.

title: PinPoint: Prompting with Informative Interior Points
summary: Modern referring image segmentation pipelines couple a vision-language model (VLM) for grounding with a promptable segmenter such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for mask generation. Prior training-free instances of this recipe consistently trail fine-tuned and reinforcement-learning (RL)-tuned specialists, and it has been unclear whether the gap comes from the VLM's grounding, SAM's capacity, or the prompt. We show that the gap is dominated by prompt ambiguity: a VLM-proposed bounding box (bbox) leaves SAM to guess which pixels inside the bbox belong to the object the expression denotes. Interior points are the natural disambiguator, but where they fall matters; prior work relies on naively sampled points that land on boundaries, distractors, and background clutter, and can even hurt performance compared to the bbox alone. Supervised and RL-tuned methods close this gap by training a VLM to predict better points; we show that this training is unnecessary. At a matched budget of five interior points, replacing naive sampling with stable, informative point selection improves cumulative Intersection-over-Union (cIoU) by 12-18 points across RefCOCO/+/g, with every model fixed. We turn this observation into PinPoint, a deterministic, training-free point selector that fuses four visual cues into a consensus map, selects compact, spatially diverse points away from boundaries, and uses the frozen VLM to label each point. Without any task-specific training, PinPoint matches supervised and RL-tuned specialists on the same stack while issuing only two VLM calls per query.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PinPoint, a training-free point selection method for improving referring image segmentation systems, achieving results comparable to supervised and RL-tuned models.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language model (VLM) and promptable segmenter combinations, like SAM, underperform when compared to fine-tuned specialist models for referring image segmentation. The motivation is to bridge this gap without requiring task-specific training.
AI.method: The authors identify that ambiguous prompts, especially in bounding box regions, hinder mask generation performance. PinPoint, their proposed method, deterministically selects stable and informative interior points using a fusion of visual cues presented in a consensus map. These points are spatially diverse and avoid object boundaries.
AI.result: PinPoint improves cumulative Intersection-over-Union (cIoU) by 12-18 points on RefCOCO/+/g datasets, using only five interior points and two calls to the VLM per query. It eliminates the need for supervised training or RL tuning.
AI.conclusion: PinPoint shows that high performance in referring image segmentation can be achieved without task-specific training. Its training-free, point-selection approach effectively closes the performance gap in systems combining VLM and promptable segmenters.

title: Near-Optimal Regret in Adversarial Kernel Bandits
summary: We study the adversarial kernel bandit problem, in which the loss at each round is induced by an arbitrary bounded element of a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS). We propose an exponential-weights algorithm built on a regularized importance-weighted loss estimator, together with an explicit correction term that cancels the bias introduced by the regularization. Our main result bounds the regret by $\widetilde{O}\big(\sqrt{T\, d_*(λ)\,\log|{X}|}\big)$, where $d_*(λ)$ is a widely-adopted notion of effective dimension that captures the complexity of the kernel. Up to logarithmic factors, this matches the known rate achieved in the related stochastic kernel bandit problem. A notable application is the Matérn$(ν,d)$ kernel with smoothness parameter $ν$ on $\mathbb{R}^d$, for which our bound specializes to $\widetilde{O}\big(T^{(ν+d)/(2ν+d)}\big)$, improving over the best-known prior rate of Chatterji et al. [2019] while simultaneously removing the rank-one adversary assumption required by their analysis. Moreover, this rate is the same as the known optimal rate for stochastic kernel bandits, and also matches a lower bound from concurrent work up to a $\log T$ factor.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an exponential-weights algorithm for adversarial kernel bandits, achieving a regret rate that matches the stochastic case up to logarithmic terms.
AI.motivation: To enhance regret bounds and eliminate assumptions in adversarial kernel bandit problems involving a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS).
AI.method: Developed an exponential-weights algorithm with a regularized loss estimator and a correction term to address regularization bias.
AI.result: Achieved a regret bound of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{T \cdot d_*(\lambda) \cdot \log |X|})$, which matches stochastic kernel bandit rates and improves on previous works.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance, removing prior constraints and matching theoretical lower bounds up to logarithmic factors.

title: 2-ASP(Q) programs with weak constraints: Complexity and efficient implementation
summary: ASP(Q) extends Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Quantifiers over answer sets. In this paper we focus on the class of ASP(Q) programs with two quantifiers and weak constraints, denoted as 2-ASP(Q)^w. 2-ASP(Q)^w is a practically relevant fragment of ASP(Q) that is expressive enough to capture optimization problems up to the class Delta_3^P. On the theoretical side, we provide a complete complexity characterization of the main computational tasks for 2-ASP(Q)^w programs, including tight completeness results and the analysis of nontrivial cases that have not been addressed in previous works. On the practical side, we introduce novel strategies for computing (optimal) quantified answer sets in the Casper system, that rely on a Counterexample-Guided Abstraction Refinement (CEGAR) technique tailored to ASP(Q). An experimental evaluation on hard benchmarks from different application domains shows that the proposed techniques are effective in practice.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 2-ASP(Q)^w, a fragment of ASP(Q) extended with quantifiers and weak constraints, establishes computational complexity results, and proposes practical optimization techniques using CEGAR in the Casper system.
AI.motivation: To extend the capabilities of Answer Set Programming (ASP) to support quantifiers and weak constraints, enabling it to handle optimization problems in higher complexity classes like Delta_3^P.
AI.method: Theoretical complexity analysis of 2-ASP(Q)^w tasks and practical implementation strategies using Counterexample-Guided Abstraction Refinement (CEGAR) in the Casper system.
AI.result: Tight complexity characterizations for computational tasks related to 2-ASP(Q)^w and effective experimental results showcasing the optimization techniques in real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The study bridges theoretical advances with practical applications, demonstrating that 2-ASP(Q)^w and its implementation in Casper are effective in handling complex optimization tasks.

title: PashtoTTS-Bench: automated screening for low-resource non-Latin-script text-to-speech
summary: Text-to-speech (TTS) evaluation for low-resource non-Latin-script languages can fail when it relies on a single ASR round-trip word error rate (WER). A system may produce no audio, speak a neighbouring language, preserve target script text only in an ASR transcript, or sound unnatural to native listeners. We introduce INSV (Intelligibility, Naturalness, Script fidelity, and Verification), a reporting framework that separates these cases. This paper reports INSV-A, the automated screening subset: synthesis completion, ASR WER/CER, transcript Script Fidelity Rate, and audio language identification. Native MOS and phonetic annotation are specified but not claimed in this release. We instantiate INSV-A as PashtoTTS-Bench, a dated benchmark for Pashto TTS. The April-May 2026 run evaluates Edge GulNawaz, Edge Latifa, OmniVoice clone, OmniVoice auto, and an Urdu negative control on 200 FLEURS and 200 filtered Common Voice 24 prompts. Under the independent omniASR_CTC_300M_v2, OmniVoice auto has the lowest WER (24.1% FLEURS, 27.4% CV24), followed by Edge GulNawaz (32.8%, 39.5%), Edge Latifa (35.6%, 47.7%), and OmniVoice clone (45.4%, 34.8%). WER below the natural-speech baseline reflects clean synthetic audio and should not be read as better than native speech. Whisper Large V3 returns 0.0% Pashto labels on checked Pashto TTS audio, while MMS-LID-4017 and SpeechBrain VoxLingua107 separate Pashto outputs from the Urdu control. The release provides provider metadata, per-sentence scores, LID audits, failure logs, and scripts for adding systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the INSV evaluation framework for assessing TTS systems targeting low-resource non-Latin-script languages, focusing on intelligibility, naturalness, script fidelity, and verification. Key metrics include synthesis completion and audio identification.
AI.motivation: TTS evaluation in low-resource non-Latin-script languages often encounters challenges like unnatural audio or incorrect language production, calling for better evaluation frameworks beyond single ASR WER metrics.
AI.method: The INSV framework categorizes key evaluation aspects of TTS systems into automated (INSV-A) and other manual dimensions. They provide benchmarks and metrics, such as PashtoTTS-Bench, for examining systems like Edge and OmniVoice using prompts and ASR tools.
AI.result: From the April-May 2026 evaluation run, results showed OmniVoice auto achieving the lowest WER, followed by Edge systems. Audio language identification tools separated Pashto data from Urdu controls, with comprehensive metadata and audits provided.
AI.conclusion: This paper proposes an improved evaluation design for TTS systems with INSV, which effectively identifies shortcomings overlooked by previous metrics. The benchmarks enable systematic screening for synthesis quality in diverse, low-resource scenarios.

title: Rotation-Invariant Spherical Watermarking via Third-Order SO(3) Representation Coupling
summary: Reliable watermarking of panoramic imagery is fundamentally challenged by arbitrary 3D rotations. As panoramas are defined on the sphere, they naturally transform under the action of $SO(3)$, rendering conventional planar representations and augmentation-based robustness strategies inadequate and devoid of theoretical guarantees. To address this, we formulate panoramas as spherical signals and leverage $SO(3)$ representation theory to derive provably rotation-invariant descriptors. While spherical harmonic coefficients transform equivariantly under rotations, the natural invariant constructions are typically limited to zeroth-order statistics which eliminate directional information and severely constrain embedding capacity. In this work, we introduce a principled third-order invariant construction by coupling higher-order $SO(3)$ irreducible representations via tensor products and projecting onto the trivial representation. This yields a spherical invariant bispectrum that preserves phase information while remaining strictly rotation-invariant. Leveraging this property, we embed watermarks into higher-order spherical harmonic coefficients and recover them from invariant bispectral scalars, enabling reliable extraction under arbitrary 3D rotations. We provide a theoretical proof of $SO(3)$ invariance for it and demonstrate experimentally its near-perfect robustness to continuous rotations while maintaining high visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of reliably watermarking panoramic imagery, which undergoes rotations in 3D space.
AI.motivation: Panoramas, represented as spherical signals, transform under $SO(3)$ (3D rotations), making conventional planar and augmentation-based watermarking approaches inadequate.
AI.method: The authors utilize $SO(3)$ representation theory to build provably rotation-invariant descriptors for panoramas. They introduce a third-order invariant construction called the spherical invariant bispectrum, which couples higher-order irreducible representations to preserve rotational invariance and phase information.
AI.result: The proposed method demonstrates theoretical invariance to $SO(3)$ and achieves near-perfect robustness under 3D rotations while maintaining high visual quality.
AI.conclusion: The approach allows reliable watermark embedding and extraction in spherical images, overcoming the limitations of traditional methods affected by 3D rotations.

title: Alignment Tampering: How Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback Is Exploited to Optimize Misaligned Biases
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026, Source code: https://alignment-tampering.github.io/
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is the standard method to align Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences. In this work, we introduce alignment tampering, a potential vulnerability where the LLM undergoing alignment influences the preference dataset, causing RLHF to amplify undesired behaviors. This arises from core limitations of RLHF: (1) preference datasets are constructed from the LLM's own outputs, allowing it to influence them, and (2) pairwise comparisons only indicate which response is better, not why. These limitations can be exploited to cause alignment tampering. For example, if an LLM generates biased responses with higher quality, annotators will prefer them based on quality. However, preference labels do not distinguish quality from bias, and the reward model inherits this limitation. Optimizing such rewards through reinforcement learning or best-of-N sampling can amplify misaligned biases. Our experiments demonstrate amplification across diverse biases: from keyword bias to propaganda (e.g., sexism), brand promotion, and instrumental goal-seeking. Mitigation remains challenging, as existing techniques for robust RLHF fail to fully resolve alignment tampering without sacrificing response quality. These findings reveal structural vulnerabilities of current RLHF and emphasize the need to prevent this vulnerability. Project page: https://alignment-tampering.github.io/

AI.tldr: This paper identifies 'alignment tampering,' a vulnerability where Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) may inadvertently amplify biases in Large Language Models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: To explore and address potential vulnerabilities in RLHF, which may lead to unintended behaviors and biases in aligning LLMs with human preferences.
AI.method: The authors identify two core limitations of RLHF—LLMs influencing received feedback and lack of distinction between response quality and biases in ranking datasets. They conduct experiments to demonstrate how these limitations amplify various biases in different scenarios.
AI.result: Experiments show that RLHF can amplify biases such as sexism, brand promotion, propaganda, and instrumental goal-seeking. Current mitigation methods prove insufficient without compromising response quality.
AI.conclusion: Current RLHF methods have structural vulnerabilities, particularly 'alignment tampering,' that allow biases to be reinforced and amplified. Preventative measures are necessary to address this issue effectively.

title: Prompt Injection Detection is Regime-Dependent: A Deployment-Aware Evaluation with Interpretable Structural Signals
summary: Prompt injection poses a critical threat to the safe deployment of large language models, yet existing detection approaches are typically evaluated under limited settings that do not reflect real-world operating constraints. In this work, we present a deployment-aware evaluation of prompt injection detection using a multi-model and multi-regime experimental framework. We compare lexical, semantic, structural, and transformer-based detectors across multiple out-of-distribution settings, repeated data splits, and both ranking and thresholded deployment metrics. We introduce interpretable structural signals that capture hierarchy overrides, system prompt spoofing, role redefinition, and evasion patterns, and assess their contribution both within sparse models and in combination with strong encoder baselines. Our results show that detection performance is highly regime-dependent and sensitive to threshold selection, with no single model dominating across all settings. Transformer-based models achieve the strongest overall performance, while structural signals provide modest but consistent gains in certain regimes and improve low false positive rate behaviour in harder scenarios. These findings highlight the gap between ranking performance and deployment effectiveness and underscore the importance of evaluating prompt injection defences under realistic operational constraints. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates various methods for detecting prompt injection attacks on large language models under real-world deployment constraints, finding that performance depends on setting, thresholding, and model type.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in effective detection of prompt injection attacks, particularly under realistic deployment conditions where existing methods fall short.
AI.method: The study uses a multi-model and multi-regime evaluation framework to test lexical, semantic, structural, and transformer-based detectors on metrics for ranking and deployment, introducing interpretable structural signals for enhanced detection.
AI.result: Transformer-based detectors performed best overall, while structural signals provided consistent gains in certain scenarios, especially where low false positive rates are critical.
AI.conclusion: Detection methods need to be evaluated under realistic, operational constraints, as effectiveness varies by regime and thresholding; no universal model excels in all settings.

title: METATR: A Multilingual, Evolving Benchmark for Automatic Text Recognition
summary: Benchmarks that reflect the diversity and complexity of real-world documents are essential for accurately evaluating Automatic Text Recognition (ATR) systems, especially Vision-Large Language Models (vLLMs). Although recent models demonstrate impressive performance, they are often evaluated on datasets containing modern, printed texts mostly written in English, which limits their relevance to many practical applications. Therefore, selecting a model for a specific use case requires evaluating it on data that matches the target documents. This highlights the importance of representative benchmarks for real-world applications. In this paper, we introduce METATR (v1.0), a multilingual, evolving benchmark designed to evaluate ATR models across a wide range of documents, facilitating meaningful model comparison and selection. The benchmark was designed to maximize diversity by including documents from various public collections. These documents cover 29 languages and include texts with multiple scripts and layouts. Beyond the dataset itself, METATR defines a standardized prompting and normalization methodology and establishes a dynamic evaluation framework. This approach is intended to produce reproducible results while remaining extensible over time. We evaluated a wide range of state-of-the-art systems, including open-source models and closed-source models. Results are reported across various dimensions, including performance at the dataset and language levels, robustness to handwritten documents, and computational efficiency. Our findings show that, although proprietary models achieve the most consistent performance, substantial variability persists across scripts and layouts. Overall, METATR provides a multidimensional, practitioner-oriented framework for assessing multilingual ATR in real-world conditions and tracking progress as the field evolves.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces METATR, a multilingual, evolving benchmark for Automatic Text Recognition (ATR) systems aimed at real-world evaluation of Vision-Large Language Models (vLLMs).
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for ATR systems mainly focus on modern, printed texts in English, which are insufficient for evaluating models in diverse real-world scenarios.
AI.method: The METATR benchmark maximizes diversity by incorporating documents in 29 languages from varied public collections. It includes standardized prompting, normalization, and a dynamic evaluation framework for reproducible and extensible assessments.
AI.result: The benchmark evaluates open-source and proprietary ATR systems, revealing that proprietary models perform more consistently but struggle with variations in scripts and layouts. It also considers robustness to handwritten documents and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: METATR provides a practitioner-oriented framework for a comprehensive evaluation of multilingual ATR, enabling performance tracking under real-world and evolving conditions.

title: Geometry-Aware Contrastive Learning for Few-Shot Automatic Modulation Recognition
summary: Standard Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) for Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR) struggles with ineffective isotropic augmentations, spectral instability, and semantic drift. To address these challenges, we propose Dynamic-Consistency Contrastive Learning (DyCo-CL), a geometry-aware framework that couples Virtual Adversarial Augmentation (VAA) with a semantic consistency loss. We provide a theoretical analysis indicating that this strategy acts as an implicit spectral regularizer for the encoder, enabling stable manifold exploration. Complementing this, our Signal-Adaptive Swin Backbone with fixed-window attention improves structural stability by constraining attention locality, while a Hybrid Knowledge Fusion module anchors representations with physical priors. Experiments on RML benchmarks show that DyCo-CL achieves a 6.27% accuracy gain in 1-shot settings over prior methods.

AI.tldr: Proposal of a new framework, DyCo-CL, to tackle limitations in self-supervised learning for automatic modulation recognition, showing significant accuracy improvements.
AI.motivation: To address issues like ineffective augmentations, spectral instability, and semantic drift in SSL for AMR.
AI.method: Introduced DyCo-CL, incorporating Virtual Adversarial Augmentation and semantic consistency loss, supported by theoretical spectral regularization and enhanced by a Signal-Adaptive Swin Backbone and Hybrid Knowledge Fusion module.
AI.result: DyCo-CL achieved 6.27% improvement in accuracy for 1-shot settings on RML benchmarks compared to previous methods.
AI.conclusion: DyCo-CL demonstrates that combining geometry-aware augmentations and consistency losses can significantly improve SSL in AMR.

title: Natural Language Query to Configuration for Retrieval Agents
summary: Modern retrieval agents expose many configuration choices -- LLM, retriever, number of documents, number of hops, and synthesis strategy -- each shaping both answer quality and serving cost. Today, these pipelines are typically hand-tuned once per workload, leaving substantial per-query optimization untapped. We formulate the problem: given a natural-language query and either an accuracy or a budget target, select from a predefined pipeline catalog the configuration that minimizes cost or maximizes accuracy at inference time. We propose **BRANE**, which uses an LLM to convert each query into workload-specific characteristics, then trains a lightweight per-configuration predictor that estimates whether the pipeline will answer the query correctly. At inference time, **BRANE** selects the configuration that maximizes predicted correctness penalized by cost, exposing a tunable cost-quality tradeoff without retraining. Across MuSiQue, BrowseComp-Plus, and FinanceBench, **BRANE** consistently pushes the cost-quality Pareto frontier, matches the best fixed configuration's accuracy at up to 89% lower cost, and outperforms LLM-routing, rule-based, and fine-tuned Qwen3-4B baselines. These results show that per-query configuration of the full retrieval pipeline is a practical alternative to static workload-level tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BRANE, a system to optimize the configuration of retrieval pipelines on a per-query basis to balance accuracy and cost effectively.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency of static, workload-level tuning in retrieval pipelines, which leaves substantial per-query cost and quality optimizations unrealized.
AI.method: BRANE uses an LLM to characterize queries, trains per-configuration predictors to estimate pipeline correctness, and selects the most cost-effective and accurate pipeline configuration at inference time.
AI.result: BRANE outperforms fixed configurations and baselines by improving cost-effectiveness and maintaining accuracy, achieving up to 89% cost reduction across datasets like MuSiQue, BrowseComp-Plus, and FinanceBench.
AI.conclusion: Per-query configuration via BRANE is a practical and superior alternative to static tuning, offering a tunable cost-quality tradeoff without requiring retraining.

title: Cast a Wider Net: Coordinated Pass@K Policy Optimization for Code Reasoning
comment: Code reasoning; pass@K optimization; coordinated planning; verifiable rewards; strategy diversity
summary: Repeated sampling with a verifier is the standard way to allocate test-time compute for code generation, with pass@$K$ as the canonical metric. Yet the standard policy class draws $K$ independent samples from a single answer distribution, so attempts often collapse onto near-duplicate reasoning paths and waste the budget on redundant rollouts. This failure is costly in competitive programming, where many problems admit multiple distinct algorithmic strategies and pass@$K$ requires only one correct attempt. We propose Coordinated Pass@$K$ Policy Optimization (CPPO), which turns pass@$K$ generation into joint exploration over strategies: a planner emits a tuple of $K{=}4$ alternative high-level methods, and a shared solver attempts one solution per method. CPPO trains this joint policy with a multiplicative planner reward, $R_{\mathrm{plan}} = J_ψ\cdot R_{\mathrm{out}}$, assigning credit only to valid strategy tuples that lead to verifier-confirmed pass@$K$ success. Across APPS, CodeContests, and LiveCodeBench-v6, CPPO improves pass@$4$ over direct sampling, planning baselines, planner-only SFT, and pass@$K$-oriented RL under the same $K{=}4$ solver-attempt budget, with statistically significant gains on six of nine model--benchmark cells. The largest single gain is $+0.16$ on Qwen3.5-9B LiveCodeBench-v6 over the strongest baseline, PKPO ($0.588 \rightarrow 0.748$; paired bootstrap, $p < 0.05$).

AI.tldr: The paper identifies inefficiencies in test-time sampling for code generation and introduces Coordinated Pass@$K$ Policy Optimization (CPPO) to address them, demonstrating notable gains in performance metrics.
AI.motivation: Efficient code generation is hindered by redundant sampling, especially in domains like competitive programming where multiple distinct solutions are possible but the existing sampling strategies often waste computational resources.
AI.method: The proposed CPPO framework coordinates strategy generation and solution attempts by employing a planner-solver architecture. It trains the system with a multiplicative planner reward scheme, favoring diverse and validated solutions.
AI.result: CPPO significantly improves pass@$4$ on multiple benchmarks (e.g., APPS, CodeContests) over standard and state-of-the-art methods, achieving a substantial 0.16 improvement on Qwen3.5-9B LiveCodeBench-v6.
AI.conclusion: CPPO demonstrates the effectiveness of coordinated sampling and strategic diversity for test-time code generation, offering a statistically superior alternative to current approaches.

title: Joint 2D-3D Segmentation and Association in Street-level Imaging
comment: 15 pages, 6 image figures, 1 in-body table, 1 in-body algorithm, 2 indexes with tables
summary: Accurate interpretation of street-level imagery is essential for large-scale urban mapping and the creation of Spatial Digital Twin (SDT) environments. This work presents a unified framework for joint 2D-3D segmentation and association that integrates visual semantics with multi-view geometric reasoning. Unlike conventional approaches that rely heavily on sequential frames for temporal tracking, our method leverages zero-shot detection and segmentation together with structure-from-motion reconstruction to establish stable cross-view correspondences. A 3D-driven association mechanism replaces traditional 2D multi-object tracking, using geometric consistency to guide identity preservation across wide-baseline viewpoints and varying imaging conditions. By combining 2D texture cues with global 3D context, the proposed pipeline is well-suited for scalable street-level processing and can be used for a variety of object types. Experiments demonstrate substantially improved coverage of ground-truth sequences and more robust identity retention compared to state-of-the-art 2D-only tracking methods, achieving a 22% performance gain in challenging urban scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a unified framework for joint 2D-3D segmentation and object association to improve accuracy in urban mapping through Spatial Digital Twin environments.
AI.motivation: To enhance urban mapping and creation of Spatial Digital Twin environments by overcoming limitations of traditional 2D tracking methods in dynamic, wide-baseline urban conditions.
AI.method: The method integrates zero-shot detection and segmentation with structure-from-motion for geometric reasoning, replacing 2D multi-object tracking with a 3D-driven association mechanism guided by geometric consistency.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates improved coverage of ground-truth data and better identity retention with a 22% performance gain in urban environments compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach shows promise for scalable processing in street-level urban mapping, offering significant improvements over traditional 2D tracking techniques, especially in complex scenarios.

title: Spend Your Rollouts Where It Counts: Rollout Allocation for Group-Based RL Post-Training
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is the dominant paradigm for post-training large language models. However, in the online, on-policy setting, rollout generation dominates the computational cost of training. Group-based policy optimization methods compute advantages from multiple rollouts per prompt, yet they indiscriminately allocate budget to prompts with collapsed reward distributions, wasting expensive rollouts on negligible learning signals. We demonstrate that group-based updates are most effective in regimes of high reward variance. Since the policy evolves throughout training, prompt informativeness must be estimated online rather than precomputed, but exhaustively evaluating every prompt is computationally prohibitive. We introduce Pilot-Commit, a budget-aware rollout allocation framework for group-based RL post-training. Pilot-Commit decouples prompt evaluation from exploitation: a pilot stage estimates per-prompt informativeness using a fraction of the budget, and the remaining rollouts are allocated to high-leverage prompts while low-signal prompts are skipped. Across multiple math reasoning benchmarks and model scales from 1.5B to 14B parameters, Pilot-Commit matches baseline accuracy with significantly lower sampling costs, reaching target accuracy up to $1.9\times$ faster than GRPO and $4.0\times$ faster than DAPO in cumulative rollouts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework called Pilot-Commit for efficient rollout allocation in group-based reinforcement learning (RL). It achieves comparable accuracy while reducing computational costs and achieving faster results.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency in group-based policy optimization during RL, where computational resources are wasted on prompts with low reward variance, leading to negligible learning outcomes.
AI.method: The authors propose Pilot-Commit, a two-stage framework. The pilot stage estimates the informativeness of prompts using a fraction of the budget, while the commit stage allocates the remaining rollouts to high-leverage prompts, bypassing low-signal ones.
AI.result: Pilot-Commit demonstrates significant efficiency improvements in math reasoning benchmarks, achieving target accuracy up to 1.9x faster than GRPO and 4.0x faster than DAPO, with reduced sampling costs.
AI.conclusion: Pilot-Commit effectively optimizes resource allocation during RL training and maintains accuracy while significantly reducing computational burdens.

title: MUSE-Autoskill: Self-Evolving Agents via Skill Creation, Memory, Management, and Evaluation
comment: 30 pages, 8 figures, 13 tables, working in progress
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents rely on reusable skills to solve complex tasks. However, existing skill creation approaches treat skills as isolated and static artifacts, limiting their reusability, reliability, and long-term improvement. We propose MUSE-Autoskill Agent (Memory-Utilizing Skill Evolution), a skill-centric agent framework that lets agents continuously improve their task-solving capability by creating, reusing, and refining skills under a unified lifecycle (creation, memory, management, evaluation, and refinement). Our framework enables agents to create skills on demand, store and reuse them across tasks, organize and select them efficiently, and evaluate them through unit tests and runtime feedback for continuous refinement. We further introduce skill-level memory that accumulates experience for each skill across tasks, enabling more effective reuse and adaptation over time. Experiments on SkillsBench provide initial evidence that lifecycle-managed skills can improve task success, efficiency, reuse, and cross-agent transfer, highlighting the importance of treating skills as long-lived, experience-aware, and testable assets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MUSE-Autoskill Agent, a framework allowing language model agents to improve task-solving by dynamically creating, refining, and reusing skills within a lifecycle-based system.
AI.motivation: Current approaches treat LLM skills as static and isolated, which limits their potential for improvement, reusability, and scalability over time.
AI.method: The authors propose a lifecycle framework for skills, involving stages like creation, memory storage, management, evaluation through tests and feedback, and continuous refinement, along with skill-level memory for improved adaptation.
AI.result: Initial experiments on a benchmark named SkillsBench show that implementing lifecycle-managed skills enhances task success, efficiency, skill reuse, and the ability to transfer skills across agents.
AI.conclusion: Skills should be considered dynamic, long-term, testable assets to maximize their reliability and efficiency in task-solving, with lifecycle management proving beneficial.

title: PersLitEval: Fine-grained Benchmark and Evaluation of LLMs on Persian Literature Questions
summary: Despite impressive multilingual capabilities, large language models (LLMs) remain poorly evaluated on literary knowledge in non-English languages. We introduce PersLitEval, a benchmark of 4,514 Persian literature multiple-choice questions across eight fine-grained categories spanning spelling, literary devices, grammar, vocabulary, word formation, and conceptual understanding, sourced from materials for the Konkur university entrance examination. We evaluate six LLMs across ten prompting strategies, revealing striking category-level disparities across three tiers of task difficulty: models reach higher accuracy on conceptual similarity tasks but struggle with formal linguistic analysis, with spelling and word formation proving the hardest across all models. Prompting strategy has a significant impact on performance, with explained few-shot examples yielding the best results, particularly on formal linguistic categories. An error analysis identifies three failure modes: semantic comprehension gaps, formal linguistic knowledge gaps, and counting/enumeration errors, suggesting that different categories require different improvement strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark, PersLitEval, to analyze Persian literature knowledge in LLMs, showing that while models excel in some areas, they struggle with formal linguistic tasks, and prompting strategy significantly affects performance.
AI.motivation: Poor evaluation of LLMs on non-English literary knowledge, particularly Persian literary capabilities, prompted the need for a specialized benchmark.
AI.method: Designed PersLitEval with 4,514 Persian literature questions across eight categories; evaluated six LLMs using ten prompting strategies to identify performance trends.
AI.result: LLMs perform better on conceptual tasks but poorly on formal linguistic analysis like spelling and word formation. Few-shot prompting yields best performance. Three failure modes were identified: semantic issues, gaps in linguistic knowledge, and enumeration errors.
AI.conclusion: PersLitEval highlights strengths and weaknesses of LLMs in Persian literature. Improving performance will require targeted strategies depending on task category.

title: Learning Reference-Guided Exposure Correction with Hybrid Illumination Characteristics
summary: We present HICNet, a reference-guided exposure correction framework. A lightweight, content-agnostic encoder distills each image into a compact illumination embedding capturing regional brightness, edge contrast, and higher-order luminance moments. The embedding difference between a source and its reference drives a multi-scale modulation network that combines FiLM-based global adjustment with Photometric Channel Rebalancing for fine-grained, illumination-aware spectral gating, producing exposure-matched outputs while faithfully preserving scene details. A cross-batch contrastive loss orders the illumination manifold, bolstering robustness to diverse lighting conditions. Trained without ground truth or intrinsic decomposition, HICNet attains better accuracy on public benchmarks and generalizes well to entirely unseen scenes.

AI.tldr: HICNet is a framework designed for reference-guided exposure correction using compact embeddings and multi-scale networks to adjust illumination, achieving robust results without ground truth training.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to create a framework for correcting image exposure effectively while preserving scene details under diverse lighting, without relying on ground truth or intrinsic decompositions.
AI.method: HICNet uses a lightweight encoder to derive a compact illumination embedding of an image. It employs a multi-scale modulation network combining FiLM global adjustments and Photometric Channel Rebalancing for precise exposure corrections. A contrastive loss ensures robustness to varying lighting conditions.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates improved accuracy on public benchmarks and shows strong generalization to unseen scenes without requiring ground truth data.
AI.conclusion: HICNet provides a novel and robust method for exposure correction that preserves scene integrity and performs well in varied and unseen lighting conditions, highlighting its potential application in diverse scenarios.

title: PIDM-DP: Physics-Informed Diffusion with Dormand-Prince Integration for Chaotic System Identification and State Reconstruction across Multiple Dynamical Regimes
comment: extended work of my journal paper submission
summary: Reconstructing continuous state trajectories of chaotic dynamical systems from sparse, noisy observations remains a fundamental open problem in nonlinear science. We introduce the Physics-Informed Diffusion Model with Dormand-Prince Integration (PIDM-DP), which embeds a fully differentiable 5th-order Dormand-Prince (DP-RK45) ODE integrator directly into the reverse sampling loop of a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). At each denoising step, physics residuals are back-propagated via automatic differentiation, constraining every generated trajectory to satisfy the system's governing equations to 5th-order accuracy. A linear-scheduled guidance mechanism that ramps the physics weight from zero at high noise levels to its full value near the clean-data limit prevents the gradient explosions that cause naive physics-informed approaches to fail on stiff systems with Jacobian eigenvalues of order $O(10^3)$. Evaluated across five benchmark systems of increasing complexity 3D Lorenz, 3D Rössler, 5D Hyperchaotic, 20D Lorenz-96, and the stiff 3D Rabinovich-Fabrikant at 10% observation density with additive Gaussian noise ($σ=0.05$), PIDM-DP achieves reconstruction RMSE improvements of up to $15.4\times$ over an unconstrained diffusion baseline and decisively outperforms the Ensemble Kalman Filter on stiff systems where ensemble covariance collapses. On the Rabinovich-Fabrikant out-of-distribution benchmark, PIDM-DP attains RMSE $0.1097 \pm 0.0269$ versus $0.9443 \pm 0.5288$ (unconstrained diffusion, $8.6\times$ worse) and $0.3561 \pm 0.3040$ (EnKF, $3.2\times$ worse), with $p<0.001$ in paired Wilcoxon tests ($N = 30$). Topological validation via the Rosenstein Lyapunov estimator confirms that PIDM-DP preserves the chaotic invariant measure.
