The framework further provides diagnostic signatures distinguishing spurious from genuine generalization: in a previously reported MNIST grokking example and an epoch-wise double descent example, apparent delayed or non-monotone generalization is shown to arise from representation degradation and readout misalignment induced by non-standard training recipes. Together, these results establish the representation-readout decomposition as a top-down framework for understanding learning dynamics and revealing underlying algorithms for interpretability research.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework for task-agnostic analysis of generalization phenomena in neural networks, analyzing representation learning and readout calibration dynamics.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a task-agnostic diagnostic framework for explaining anomalous generalization phenomena like grokking and epoch-wise double descent across diverse tasks and architectures.
AI.method: Using representational geometry, neural tangent kernels, and linear probing to analyze learning dynamics via representation learning and readout calibration.
AI.result: The analysis revealed that both representation learning and readout calibration are active throughout training and explains grokking and epoch-wise double descent phenomena. Diagnostic signals distinguishing spurious from genuine generalization were also identified.
AI.conclusion: The representation-readout decomposition offers a generalized framework for understanding and diagnosing complex learning dynamics in neural networks, aiding interpretability research.

title: How and What to Imagine? Visual Thinking in Unified Multimodal Models for Cross-View Spatial Reasoning
summary: Cross-view spatial reasoning remains a weak spot for vision-language models (VLMs): they often reason in language and lose the fine-grained geometry needed for the task. Thinking with images aims to address this by generating an intermediate thinking image, but recent work shows that models often ignore the visual evidence in these traces. We therefore ask how to make visual thinking matter, and what kind of visual thinking works best. We study these questions in unified multimodal models (UMMs), which natively support interleaved image-text generation. For the first question, we propose View Dropout (VDrop), a training-time intervention that hides parts of one input view from the answer span while keeping them visible to the thinking-image tokens. This encourages the model to use the thinking image when answering, instead of relying only on the input views. Once the thinking image is used for answer prediction, we study which type of visual thinking is most effective. We frame this as a learnability-informativeness tradeoff and compare three thinking-image variants: top-down, panoramic, and point-matching renderings. Trained on synthetic scenes and evaluated on five real-world out-of-domain benchmarks, panoramic visual thinking with VDrop is the only configuration that is both informative and learnable, and it achieves the best out-of-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: This study addresses the challenge of improving visual reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs) by proposing techniques that encourage models to utilize visual evidence effectively during reasoning.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enhance cross-view spatial reasoning in VLMs, as existing models tend to rely heavily on language reasoning and lack fine-grained geometric understanding necessary for certain tasks.
AI.method: The paper introduces 'View Dropout' (VDrop), a training intervention that hides parts of one input view from the answer while keeping them visible to intermediate 'thinking image' tokens. It also evaluates the effectiveness of different thinking-image types, including top-down, panoramic, and point-matching renderings.
AI.result: The research finds that panoramic visual thinking combined with VDrop achieves the best out-of-domain generalization among the evaluated methods, balancing informativeness and learnability.
AI.conclusion: Encouraging models to utilize visual evidence with approaches like VDrop and panoramic visual thinking can significantly enhance reasoning performance in VLMs, especially for out-of-domain tasks.

title: Q-GeoMem: Question-Guided Geometric Memory for Video Spatial Reasoning
summary: Video spatial reasoning requires accumulating viewpoint-dependent evidence over time while retaining information useful to the question being asked. Existing spatial video-language models improve geometric perception and long-range context modeling, but often treat memory as a generic temporal cache, which can introduce redundant or irrelevant geometry and weaken long-horizon reasoning. We propose \textbf{\ours}, a question-guided geometric memory framework for video spatial reasoning. \ours injects camera-conditioned geometry into visual tokens and maintains two complementary memories: a Fine-Grained Context Bank for recent dense features and camera states, and a Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank for compact long-range evidence. Each candidate frame is scored by the product of Q-Former-based question relevance and novelty with respect to the retained bank; this score is stored and reused during reading, while a capacity-based replacement rule keeps the bank compact. During reasoning, both memories are read before update and adaptively fused with the current frame representation. Experiments on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench show that \ours achieves state-of-the-art performance among evaluated spatial reasoning models, validating the effectiveness of question-guided geometric memory. Ablations further verify the contribution of the proposed evidence scoring mechanism.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces \textbf{\ours}, a question-guided geometric memory framework for video spatial reasoning, which incorporates question relevance and novelty scoring to improve reasoning accuracy in long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Spatial video-language models often poorly handle long-horizon reasoning because they treat memory as a generic temporal cache, storing redundant or irrelevant geometric information.
AI.method: \ours leverages a dual-memory system: a Fine-Grained Context Bank storing recent dense features and a Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank holding compact, long-range evidence. It scores candidate frames by combining a Q-Former-based relevance and novelty score, and updates memory adaptively to maintain compactness.
AI.result: The proposed \ours framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench spatial reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating superiority over existing models.
AI.conclusion: \ours effectively employs question-guided geometric memory for improved video spatial reasoning, with its design and scoring mechanism contributing significantly to its performance gains.

title: PARE: Pruning and Adaptive Routing for Efficient Video Generation
summary: Video Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) generate high-quality videos but demand substantial compute due to wide blocks, deep architectures, and iterative sampling. Recent methods reduce cost by compressing width, depth, or sampling steps, but typically commit to a fixed architecture that cannot adapt to individual inputs or denoising stages. We propose PARE (Pruning and Adaptive Routing for Efficient video generation), which jointly compresses width and depth with structure-aware pruning and input-adaptive routing. For width, we observe that attention heads specialize into spatial and temporal roles, and design importance scoring that accounts for this distinction to prevent motion-critical temporal heads from being pruned prematurely. For depth, we train a lightweight router conditioned on denoising timestep and visual content to dynamically select which blocks to execute at each step, enabling per-input compute adaptation rather than static block removal. A progressive pipeline first recovers width-pruned quality via distillation, then jointly optimizes the student and router to decouple the two learning objectives. Experiments on Wan2.1-14B for both image-to-video and text-to-video generation show that PARE substantially reduces per-step computation while preserving quality across VBench dimensions, and composes with step distillation for further acceleration.

AI.tldr: PARE optimizes video diffusion transformers' efficiency by dynamically pruning blocks and routing inputs, reducing computation without degrading quality.
AI.motivation: Video generation with diffusion transformers requires significant computational resources due to their large architectures and iterative sampling process. There is a need for adaptive methods to reduce computational costs without compromising video quality.
AI.method: PARE uses structure-aware pruning and input-adaptive routing to selectively reduce model width (temporally and spatially specialized attention heads) and depth (dynamic block selection using a router conditioned on timestep and visual content). It employs a progressive training strategy that includes knowledge distillation.
AI.result: PARE achieves substantial per-step computational reductions in video generation while maintaining output quality on various benchmarks, and further composes with step distillation for additional speed-ups.
AI.conclusion: PARE demonstrates that adaptive pruning and routing in video diffusion models can significantly enhance efficiency without sacrificing generation quality, offering a scalable solution for video synthesis.

title: Adversarial Dual On-Policy Distillation from Expressive Flow-based Teacher
summary: Learning from demonstrations in embodied control is often cast as behavioral cloning, and recent diffusion or flow-matching policies improve this paradigm by modeling multi-modal expert actions. Yet these methods remain offline supervised learners: the policy is trained only on expert states and receives no corrective signal on the states it actually visits. On-policy distillation (OPD) offers a natural remedy, but standard OPD assumes a strong fixed teacher, which is unavailable in demonstration-only control. We propose \textbf{FA-OPD}, an \emph{adversarial dual on-policy distillation} method in which a Flow Matching (FM) teacher is learned from demonstrations and co-trained with a lightweight MLP student. The teacher provides two complementary signals on student rollouts. The reward channel learns an expert-likeness objective over state-action pairs and drives online exploration through long-horizon policy optimization. The action channel supplies dense local targets at student-visited states, stabilizing exploitation. FA-OPD couples them so that reward distillation enables generalization beyond point-wise demonstrations, while action distillation keeps exploration anchored near expert-like behavior. Across six robot navigation, manipulation, and locomotion benchmarks, FA-OPD beats strong baselines and shows much stronger robustness under noisy or limited demonstrations.

AI.tldr: FA-OPD is a proposed adversarial dual on-policy distillation method that combines a Flow Matching teacher with an MLP student for learning from demonstrations in embodied control. It outperforms previous methods across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of offline supervised learning in behavioral cloning for embodied control, particularly the lack of corrective signals and reliance on strong fixed teachers.
AI.method: The method involves co-training a Flow Matching (FM) teacher with an MLP student. The teacher provides two signals during student rollouts: a reward channel for expert-likeness and exploration, and an action channel for local stabilization. These are coupled to balance generalization and exploitation.
AI.result: FA-OPD achieves stronger performance and robustness compared to existing methods, particularly under conditions with noisy or limited demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: FA-OPD represents a significant improvement in on-policy distillation for learning from demonstrations, offering a balanced and robust approach that generalizes well across tasks.

title: Towards Controllable Image Generation through Representation-Conditioned Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful tools for high-quality image generation and editing, but guiding these models to produce specific outputs remains a challenge. Conventional approaches rely on conditioning mechanisms, such as text prompts or semantic maps, which require extensively annotated datasets. In this preliminary work, we explore diffusion models conditioned on representations from a pre-trained self-supervised model. The self-conditioning mechanism not only improves the quality of unconditional image generation, but also provides a representation space that can be used to control the generation. We explore this conditioning space by identifying directions of variations, and demonstrate promising properties in terms of smoothness and disentanglement.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method for conditioning diffusion models using pre-trained self-supervised representations to improve image generation quality and control.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of guiding diffusion models for specific outputs without relying on extensively annotated datasets.
AI.method: The authors condition diffusion models on features derived from pre-trained self-supervised models and explore representation space for control and quality improvement.
AI.result: The proposed self-conditioning mechanism enhances unconditional image generation quality and results in promising control properties, such as smoothness and disentanglement.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised representations can provide effective conditioning for diffusion models, enabling high-quality image generation with promising controllability features.

title: When Eyes Betray AI: Social Gaze Consistency as a Semantic Cue for AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: Recent generative models have largely closed the gap on low-level artifacts - pixel fingerprints, frequency anomalies, upsampling traces - particularly in person-centric and partial-edit settings where the manipulated region is small and surrounded by photometrically authentic content. We introduce Social Gaze Consistency, a high-level semantic cue defined as the mutual coherence of gaze direction, head-eye alignment, and pupil placement between interacting individuals, and show that it constitutes a previously underutilized detection axis orthogonal to existing low-level paradigms. We instantiate this insight through three coupled mechanisms: (i) a controlled diagnostic dataset with region-specific perturbations of gaze-consistent imagery, where strict pair-level grouping forecloses generator-fingerprint memorization as an optimization-time shortcut rather than relying on augmentation; (ii) Block-Compositional Caption Supervision, which holds a single 5-block reasoning skeleton invariant across 1,250 macro-combined captions, decoupling reasoning consistency from surface diversity; (iii) Cross-architecture validation showing the same supervision improves a vision-language backbone (FakeVLM) by +3.7 pp on the COCOAI Interaction subset (balanced accuracy 67.8 -> 71.5) and +1.3 pp on the COCOAI Person subset (83.0 -> 84.3), with consistent gains on a vision-only backbone (Effort), evidencing a backbone-agnostic cue. Real- and fake-class recalls rise simultaneously, ruling out a "predict-all-fake" artifact. A four-step mechanistic account - paired-edit shortcut blocking, hard-to-easy difficulty transfer, CLIP prior preservation, and diffusion-family shared spectral weakness in periocular structure - explains why training on a single inpainter (FLUX.1-Fill) transfers to multi-generator suites. We will release the code upon acceptance to facilitate reproducibility.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Social Gaze Consistency, a high-level semantic cue leveraging mutual gaze coherence for improved detection of manipulations in person-centric images, complementing existing low-level artifact detection methods.
AI.motivation: Low-level generative model artifacts are becoming harder to identify due to advancements, especially in person-centric and partial-edit cases. High-level cues like gaze coherence are largely unexplored for detecting manipulations.
AI.method: The authors introduce mechanisms to operationalize Social Gaze Consistency using a diagnostic dataset with controlled gaze perturbations, Block-Compositional Caption Supervision for reasoning consistency, and cross-architecture validation for model performance evaluation.
AI.result: Applying their approach improved balanced accuracy in vision-language (FakeVLM) and vision-only (Effort) backbones, achieving gains of +3.7 pp on COCOAI Interaction subset and +1.3 pp on COCOAI Person subset. Both real- and fake-class recalls improved.
AI.conclusion: Social Gaze Consistency provides a novel, effective, and backbone-agnostic detection method for identifying manipulated visual content, demonstrating transferability across generator models and architectural setups.

title: High-Quality Synthetic Financial Time-Series using a GAN-Diffusion Framework
summary: In recent years, financial institutions and firms have increasingly adopted synthetic data to address data scarcity and to generate counterfactual market scenarios. However, reproducing all the statistical properties of financial time series, commonly known as stylized facts, remains an open challenge for many existing general-purpose architectures. In this paper, we present a quality-aware generative framework that combines two classes of generative methods, demonstrating how their integration addresses existing limitations while enhancing the realism of synthetic data. Specifically, we first introduce CoMeTS-GAN (Correlated Multivariate Time Series GAN), a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (C-GAN) designed to jointly generate mid-price and volume time-series for correlated stocks. We then show how our GAN architecture can be incorporated into state-of-the-art diffusion models to enhance the quality of generated correlation structures. Specifically, the GAN's Critic serves as a quality evaluation module that guides the diffusion process, enforcing learned correlation structures in the generated time-series. Our framework offers a lightweight and responsive solution for realistic stock market simulation, explicitly modeling inter-asset correlation structures. We experimentally validate our framework against leading generative architectures, showing that it more effectively captures the stylized facts of stock markets and models inter-asset correlations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel generative framework that integrates GANs and diffusion models to enhance the realism and correlation structures of synthetic financial time series data.
AI.motivation: Financial institutions face challenges in reproducing statistical properties (stylized facts) of financial time series in synthetic data, which limits their utility for market simulation and analysis.
AI.method: The authors introduce CoMeTS-GAN, a Conditional GAN tailored for generating multivariate time series of correlated stocks, coupled with diffusion models guided by the GAN's Critic module for enhanced correlation realism.
AI.result: The proposed framework accurately replicates stylized facts and inter-asset correlations of stock markets, outperforming leading generative architectures in experimental validations.
AI.conclusion: The study presents a lightweight, quality-aware solution for realistic market simulation, improving synthetic data generation while modeling complex inter-asset correlations.

title: Feedforward 3D Editing Learns from Semantic-Part Transformation
summary: 3D editing is a fundamental capability for scalable 3D content creation. While image editing has rapidly evolved toward large-scale feedforward generative paradigms, 3D AI generation remains dominated by training-free editing pipelines. A central challenge of feedforward 3D editing lies in the lack of high-quality paired supervision. Editable 3D assets require simultaneous preservation of geometry, multi-view consistency, structural coherence, and localized edit controllability. Existing 3D editing datasets often rely on independently generated assets, image-mediated reconstruction or narrow edit taxonomies, leading to inaccurate localization, weak preservation, blurred edit boundaries, and limited semantic consistency. In this work, we introduce a new perspective: scalable feedforward 3D editing should be learned from semantic-part transformations. Based on this insight, we propose Pxform, a high-quality 3D editing dataset with over 100K consistent before/after editing pairs across seven edit types. Instead of treating objects as unstructured shapes, our pipeline grounds edits directly in semantic 3D parts. Built upon Pxform, we further propose PartFlow, a feedforward 3D editing network that injects source-aware latent control into pretrained 3D generative priors. PartFlow introduces mask-aware velocity preservation and render-space consistency supervision to jointly improve edit fidelity and source preservation, while requiring no 3D edit mask during inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that high-quality semantic-part supervision substantially improves scalable 3D editing, enabling PartFlow to achieve state-of-the-art performance on both geometric and appearance editing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Pxform, a large-scale dataset, and PartFlow, a feedforward network, to enhance scalable 3D editing. Pxform provides semantic-part based high-quality paired data, and PartFlow improves edit fidelity and source preservation without requiring edit masks during inference.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenges of feedforward 3D editing, including the lack of paired supervision, issues with geometry and consistency preservation, and limitations with existing editing datasets.
AI.method: The authors introduce Pxform, a dataset grounded in semantic-part transformations, and PartFlow, a network that utilizes source-aware latent control, mask-aware velocity preservation, and render-space consistency to improve editing outcomes.
AI.result: PartFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance in 3D geometric and appearance editing, supported by extensive benchmarking and utilizing Pxform's high-quality paired supervision.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-part-based supervision substantially advances scalable 3D editing, and PartFlow effectively unlocks high-quality feedforward 3D transformations, outperforming existing methods.

title: DEI: Diversity in Evolutionary Inference for Quality-Diversity Search
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop Scalable Learning and Optimization for Efficient Multimodal AI Agents (SCALE)
summary: We present DEI: Diversity in Evolutionary Inference, a distributed Quality-Diversity (QD) search framework that assigns heterogeneous large language models (LLMs) as mutation operators across peer nodes communicating with non-blocking collective operations. Unlike homogeneous parallel search, which replicates a single model's inductive biases across all workers, DEI treats each LLM's distinct creative prior as a complementary source of behavioral novelty. Extending the Digital Red Queen framework with DEI, nodes share local optimal solutions at the end of each round to seed the next round's population. This creates cross-model adversarial pressure that drives robustness beyond intra-model self-play. Evaluated on the Core War domain, a competitive programming benchmark in which Redcode warrior programs battle inside a simulated machine, a four-node heterogeneous ensemble (GPT-5.4-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.2, and Claude Haiku 4.5) achieves 124 percent higher merged-archive QD-Score (45.90 vs. 20.46) and 28 percent higher coverage (80.6 percent vs. 63.0 percent of cells) than a single-node baseline at equal total LLM-call budget. The heterogeneous ensemble also outperforms an equally-budgeted homogeneous ensemble on QD-Score, coverage, and held-out solution generality across all four model families. These results provide the first empirical evidence that model diversity, not merely parallelism, is the key driver of gain in distributed LLM-based QD search.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DEI, a system leveraging diverse large language models (LLMs) as mutation operators for enhanced Quality-Diversity (QD) search, shown to outperform homogeneous setups in generating novel and robust solutions.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper was to overcome the limitations of homogeneous parallel search frameworks, where the same model bias is replicated, by utilizing the complementary creativity of diverse LLMs to enhance robustness and behavioral novelty.
AI.method: The DEI framework assigns different LLMs as creative agents across distributed nodes, which communicate through non-blocking operations. After each search round, nodes share their local best results to seed the next population, fostering cross-model adversarial pressure and collaboration.
AI.result: In tests on the Core War domain, DEI's four-node LLM ensemble achieved significantly higher Quality-Diversity scores, solution coverage, and generality compared to single-node and homogeneous setups, demonstrating the strength of diversity.
AI.conclusion: This paper concludes that model diversity, rather than parallelism alone, is the critical factor in achieving superior performance in distributed LLM-based Quality-Diversity search tasks.

title: Plans for Evaluating Structured Generative Search Summaries
summary: We propose a framework for evaluating structured generative search summaries that are placed atop organic web search results. A structured summary, generated by a large language model, typically consists of an overview, several sections with section titles, and a list of source documents that are cited within the summary. We then describe our plans for implementing and evaluating the framework.

AI.tldr: Framework introduced for assessing structured generative search summaries combining overview, sections, and citation lists.
AI.motivation: To create a standardized and effective method to evaluate structured generative summaries generated by large language models in web search contexts.
AI.method: The paper introduces a framework that assesses summaries based on formatting (overview, sections with titles, and cited sources), with implementation and evaluation plans provided.
AI.result: The framework outlines desired organizational elements for summaries and provides clarity on evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework contributes to improving the quality and efficacy of structured LLM-generated search summaries on the web.

title: Deep-layer limit and stability analysis of the basic forward-backward-splitting induced network (II): learning problems
summary: Deep unfolding neural networks derived from iterative optimization schemes and numerical ordinary/partial differential equations (ODEs/PDEs) have attracted much attention in data science over the last decade. Therein, numerous important network architectures were constructed from the basic forward-backward-splitting (FBS) algorithm. In this paper, we continue our research on the most basic FBS-induced network, an architecture unrolled from the original FBS algorithm by incorporating direct parameter relaxations. Following the difference/differential inclusion formulations in our previous forward system analyses, we here consider some theoretical aspects of corresponding learning problems. Under some mild assumptions, we establish a general convergence property of the training problem of the basic FBS-induced network to the learning problem of the deep-layer limit system, implying a $Γ$-convergence argument showing that any cluster point of the optimal learning parameters for the network is a solution to the learning problem of the deep-layer limit system. A qualitative analysis of perturbation stabilities of these learning problems is also presented. A simple numerical experiment is conducted to validate our main general convergence result.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the theoretical convergence and stability properties of deep unfolding neural networks derived from the forward-backward-splitting (FBS) algorithm.
AI.motivation: To provide theoretical insights into the convergence behavior and stability of learning problems in deep unfolding neural networks based on the FBS algorithm.
AI.method: Analyzed training problems using difference/differential inclusion formulations, established $Γ$-convergence properties, and conducted qualitative stability analysis.
AI.result: Showed general convergence of training problems to deep-layer limit systems and validated findings through a simple numerical experiment.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that optimal learning parameters for FBS-induced networks converge to solutions of deep-layer limit systems, with perturbation stability also addressed.

title: Jailbreak susceptibility prediction and mitigation via the behavioral geometry of models
summary: Evaluating and mitigating a generative system's susceptibility to jailbreak attacks is critical to its safe deployment. Given the number of deployable systems, full per-configuration evaluation and optimization is impractical. In this paper, we formalize the behavioral geometry of a population of models that, by leveraging previously evaluated and defended models, supports both efficient susceptibility prediction and effective defense transfer across a population. We apply the framework to 79 models spanning 24 providers and to 100 system configurations of a single base model. Simple methods that use the behavioral geometry reach an AUPRC of $0.94$ for susceptibility detection with $\approx98\%$ fewer probes relative to a full evaluation. Using the behavioral geometry to select which model to transfer an optimized defense from outperforms same-provider assignment ($+2\%$, $p = 0.03$) at no additional probe cost, with a set of three models sufficient to cover the population. Results are robust to hyperparameter selection and judge.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to efficiently evaluate and defend against jailbreak attacks in generative models by leveraging behavioral geometry, achieving high predictive accuracy with fewer resources.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the impracticality of full per-configuration evaluations for generative systems, aiming to enhance safety while optimizing resource use.
AI.method: The authors use a formalization of behavioral geometry across a population of models to predict susceptibility and transfer efficient defenses.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves high predictive accuracy (AUPRC of 0.94) with significantly reduced probes (~98% less) and improves defense transfer outcomes (+2%, p=0.03) among models.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral geometry offers a scalable and effective approach for evaluating and mitigating risks in generative systems, enabling safer deployments with fewer resources.

title: SpatialBench: Is Your Spatial Foundation Model an All-Round Player?
summary: While spatial foundation models have demonstrated impressive performance on standard datasets, a critical question remains: are they truly all-round players capable of generalizing robustly across diverse downstream tasks, arbitrary viewpoints, shifting scene domains, varying input densities, and specific hardware constraints? Answering this overarching question requires a holistic assessment, yet current models are mainly evaluated on specific domains for which they were specifically designed or trained. Such evaluations are intrinsically limited by narrow paradigm coverage, limited scene domains, and arbitrary frame sampling, making it fundamentally difficult to assess their true generalization capabilities. To address this gap, we present SpatialBench, a cross-paradigm, domain-diverse benchmark for spatial foundation models with deterministic sampling. SpatialBench features unprecedented scale and rigorous deterministic design, comprising 19 datasets and 546 scenes across 5 diverse spatial domains. It comprehensively evaluates 41 models across 6 paradigms on 5 task suites under 4 different input density settings. Our extensive evaluation reveals that current models are not yet all-round players, and uncovers crucial insights for future advancement. Specifically, we demonstrate that full-context attention maximizes accuracy while bounded-memory strategies unlock long-sequence scalability. Moreover, our empirical evaluations in challenging embodied and egocentric tasks demonstrate that strict domain alignment and high data quality are far more critical to performance than simple dataset scaling. Furthermore, to address the largest data gap identified in our analysis, we go beyond evaluation by introducing a large-scale dataset, DA-Next-5M, and a strong baseline model, DA-Next, pushing the boundaries of spatial representation learning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SpatialBench for holistic evaluation of spatial foundation models, revealing their shortcomings and proposing DA-Next-5M dataset and DA-Next baseline.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of comprehensive and diverse evaluation methods for assessing spatial foundation models' generalization capabilities across tasks, domains, and conditions.
AI.method: The paper develops SpatialBench, a diverse benchmark with deterministic sampling, and evaluates 41 models across 5 domains, 6 paradigms, and 5 task suites.
AI.result: Current models are found lacking in generalization, with insights identifying attention-based methods and domain alignment as crucial. A new dataset and baseline model are introduced to fill data gaps.
AI.conclusion: Spatial foundation models are not yet robust all-rounders, highlighting the need for holistic benchmarks and improvements in data alignment and methodology.

title: G3T Up! Gravity Aligned Coordinate Frames Simplify Pointmap Processing
summary: Modern feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods like VGGT predict pixel-aligned pointmaps in camera-centric coordinate frames. However, this choice of coordinate frame is not always optimal. We propose instead to predict pointmaps in upright, gravity-aligned frames that exploit strong structural cues present in many real-world scenes. Unlike camera-centric frames, gravity-aligned frames share a common vertical axis across viewpoints, reducing the rotational degrees of freedom needed to relate pointmaps to one another. To this end, we introduce the Gravity Grounded Geometry Transformer (G3T), fine-tuned from existing models on gravity-aligned 3D data. G3T produces highly accurate gravity-aware predictions, including upright pointmaps and camera-to-gravity poses. We further introduce G3T-Long, a submap-based incremental 3D reconstruction pipeline that leverages the reduced rotational degrees of freedom afforded by upright frames to achieve significantly improved reconstruction accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using gravity-aligned frames for 3D reconstruction instead of camera-centric frames, introducing the G3T and G3T-Long models for improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional 3D reconstruction methods rely on camera-centric frames, which may not fully exploit strong structural cues in real-world scenes.
AI.method: The authors propose Gravity Grounded Geometry Transformer (G3T), which predicts in gravity-aligned frames. They also introduce G3T-Long for incremental reconstruction using upright frames.
AI.result: G3T achieves accurate gravity-aware predictions, and G3T-Long leverages upright frames for better 3D reconstruction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Using gravity-aligned frames reduces rotational degrees of freedom, leading to enhanced 3D reconstruction performance with the proposed models.

title: Explainable Comparison of Feature-Based and Deep Learning Models for TROPOMI Methane Plume Screening
summary: Continuous and global detection of large methane emissions is a crucial step for global warming mitigation. Satellite observations, such as from S5P/TROPOMI, combined with plume detection algorithms, can play a key role in this effort. However, not all TROPOMI plume detections that look like methane emission plumes are the result of actual emissions. A significant part of the plume-like features in the data are retrieval artifacts. Such artifacts could be the result of variations in elevation or albedo gradients, high concentrations of aerosols, coastal lines, water bodies, etc. Previous work approached the problem of plume-artifact classification by means of a Support Vector Machine Classifier (SVC), trained on an extensive set of observation-based scalar features designed by domain experts. However, such an approach limits the information scope received by the algorithm to what is deemed to be important by the experts, breaks the spatial relationship between pixels, and loses information during the process of statistical aggregation. In this study, we compare feature-based (SVC, Random Forest, XGBoost) and image-based (ResNet-18, ResNet-34) models for methane plume-artifact classification under balanced and imbalanced evaluation settings. To interpret the results, we apply SHAP-based explainability to both model families. Our findings provide practical guidance for model selection in operational methane-screening workflows such as the CAMS Methane Hotspot Explorer.

AI.tldr: This paper compares machine learning models for classifying methane plume artifacts, highlighting differences between feature-based and image-based methods.
AI.motivation: The study aims to improve methane emissions detection by addressing inaccuracies caused by plume-like artifacts in satellite data, crucial for global warming mitigation.
AI.method: The authors compared feature-based models (SVC, Random Forest, XGBoost) and image-based models (ResNet-18, ResNet-34) for classifying methane plume artifacts. They also utilized SHAP for result explainability.
AI.result: The study provides insights into the performance of different models under balanced and imbalanced settings, offering guidance for methane-screening workflows.
AI.conclusion: Image-based models show promise in improving classification accuracy by preserving spatial relationships and reducing information loss, benefiting operational methane detection systems.

title: Symbolic Regression via Latent Iterative Refinement
summary: Symbolic regression (SR) seeks closed-form mathematical expressions that fit observed data. Neural SR methods amortize the search by training an encoder to map observations directly to expressions in a single pass, but this amortized inference leaves a residual amortization gap between its one-shot prediction and the true posterior. We propose Latent Equation Embedding (LEE), a framework that closes this gap through iterative amortized inference in a functionally grounded latent space. LEE learns a shared latent space Z equipped with three components: an encoder f_theta that jointly embeds symbolic tokens and numerical observations into a single latent vector z; an expression decoder g_expr that reconstructs formulas from z; and an evaluation decoder g_eval that predicts function values from z, explicitly grounding the latent space in functional behavior. At inference, LEE performs iterative refinement by re-encoding decoded expressions jointly with observations, progressively improving the latent estimate. LEE uses the encoder itself as a learned inference optimizer: each re-encoding step implicitly computes the mismatch between the candidate and the data. Because g_eval is differentiable in z, we additionally interleave continuous gradient descent with discrete re-encoding, yielding a hybrid iterative and gradient refinement procedure. On SRBench across three noise levels, against 19 baselines spanning genetic programming, symbolic-neural hybrids, and pre-trained Transformers, LEE produces expressions 2--10x simpler than the strongest accuracy-oriented baselines, including Operon, GP-GOMEA, TPSR, RAG-SR, and GenSR, with complexity 8--11 versus 20--90. These results advance the low-complexity region of the accuracy-complexity Pareto frontier and show graceful degradation as noise increases.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Latent Equation Embedding (LEE), a symbolic regression framework that iteratively improves function estimates for simpler and more accurate mathematical expression discovery.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in the limitations of existing neural symbolic regression methods, which suffer from an amortization gap in one-shot predictions, resulting in sub-optimal performance.
AI.method: LEE leverages an encoder-decoder architecture to embed symbolic tokens and observations into a shared latent space. It refines predictions iteratively using a hybrid process of discrete re-encoding and gradient-based updates, addressing the amortization gap.
AI.result: LEE outperforms 19 baselines across three noise levels on SRBench, producing expressions that are 2-10x simpler while maintaining accuracy. It advances towards the low-complexity region of the accuracy-complexity Pareto frontier and exhibits resilience to noise.
AI.conclusion: LEE demonstrates significant improvements in symbolic regression by reducing expression complexity and iteratively refining predictions, offering a robust and efficient solution even under noisy conditions.

title: Uniboost: Global Coordination with Value Alignment for Fair and Efficient Traffic Allocation
summary: With the rapid evolution of internet services, recommendation systems have become indispensable. In particular, the blending (re-ranking) stage plays a pivotal role in allocating traffic across diverse business objectives. However, existing approaches often suffer from coupled allocation plans, score inflation, and a lack of interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose Uniboost, a unified traffic allocation framework. Uniboost introduces a posterior value alignment mechanism that calibrates abstract model scores to anchor metrics with explicit business semantics, significantly enhancing interpretability. Furthermore, it employs an independent linear boosting paradigm to decouple complex weighting schemes, enabling precise attribution of each plan's contribution. We validate the effectiveness of Uniboost through online A/B tests and in-depth data analysis, demonstrating three key findings: 1) Reducing the overall weight of weighted scores effectively mitigates unintended business interference, yielding a more efficient micro-level traffic allocation strategy; 2) Post-hoc analyses and aggregated dashboards provide intuitive, macro-level insights that guide the design of the overall traffic allocation mechanism; 3) The proposed "Effective Completion Score" serves as an easily obtainable post-metric that offers a reliable anchor for content recommendation pipelines. Collectively, our experiments show that Uniboost not only improves traffic allocation efficiency and recommendation performance at the micro level but also provides macro-level guidance for system iteration. Thus, this work provides an efficient and controllable traffic regulation solution for large-scale industrial recommendation systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Uniboost, a unified framework addressing inefficiencies in traffic allocation for recommendation systems by enhancing interpretability, decoupling complexity, and validating effectiveness through experimentation.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in recommendation systems pertaining to coupled allocation plans, score inflation, and lack of interpretability in traffic allocation mechanisms.
AI.method: Uniboost integrates a posterior value alignment mechanism for metric calibration and employs independent linear boosting for decoupled weighting and precise contribution attribution.
AI.result: Uniboost reduces unintended interference, enables intuitive insights via dashboards, and introduces the "Effective Completion Score" as a reliable metric, validated through A/B tests and data analysis.
AI.conclusion: Uniboost improves micro-level traffic allocation and recommendation performance while providing macro guidance, offering a scalable and controllable solution for industrial scenarios.

title: AnySurf: Any Surface Generation with Directed Edge
summary: Open surface components prevail in real industrial 3D content and support rendering, physical simulation and geometric editing. Garments serve as a typical open surface type, with numerous existing generation methods leveraging sewing patterns to generate 2D panels and stitch them into 3D shapes. Such domain-specific designs lack scalability and cannot generalize to shoes and accessories. Common field-based 3D generators prioritize watertight meshes and tend to create flawed double-layer structures on open surfaces. Though Trellis2 adopts field-free representation, its open surface results still contain normal and topology errors. We present AnySurf, a unified framework generating open, closed and hybrid 3D surfaces with accurate face orientation. Built on directed-edge enhanced Flexible Dual Grid (FDG-D), our representation retains normal direction information via oriented grid edges. We also propose ROS-FT post-training and a lightweight DE-Adapter with merely 1% extra parameters, facilitating directed edge learning while preserving original generation performance. We further construct Outfit3D dataset containing industrial garments and closed accessories. Our work transforms garment modeling into a universal 3D generation task. Experimental results demonstrate superior mesh quality and better practicality for downstream applications.

AI.tldr: AnySurf is a framework for generating open, closed, and hybrid 3D surfaces, addressing challenges like normal and topology errors in traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome scalability and generalization challenges in 3D surface generation for items like garments, shoes, and accessories, and to fix issues like flawed double-layer structures in current methods.
AI.method: The method introduces a novel representation using directed-edge enhanced Flexible Dual Grid (FDG-D) to retain normal direction information. It incorporates ROS-FT post-training and a lightweight DE-Adapter to enable advanced surface generation.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates superior mesh quality and scalability, with practical applications in industrial 3D content such as garments and accessories.
AI.conclusion: AnySurf successfully transforms garment generation into a universal 3D surface generation task, enabling improved mesh quality and supporting diverse applications.

title: LUCoS: Latent Unsupervised Context Selection for Tabular Foundation Models
comment: Comments: 18 pages, 4 figures, supplementary appendices included
summary: Selecting which instances to label is a key challenge in low-label tabular learning. For recent Tabular Foundation Models such as TabPFN, context selection directly determines predictive performance. Supervised oracle experiments show that carefully chosen labeled context sets can strongly outperform random selection under the same labeling budget. However, the cold-start setting, where instances must be selected before any labels are available, has received little attention in the TFM literature. This problem is fundamentally geometric. In vision and language, foundation models induce embedding spaces where simple geometric selection methods are effective. In contrast, tabular instance selection has so far been performed predominantly in the original tabular space, which lacks a natural metric; heterogeneous types, mixed scales, and nonlinear interactions make raw-space distances unreliable for context construction, and original-space selection falls below random on the majority of datasets as the budget grows. We propose LUCoS (Latent Unsupervised Context Selection), which replaces raw-feature geometry with the latent geometry induced by embeddings from an unsupervised Prior-Fitted Network (PFN) and selects representative medoids as context. Evaluated on 67 OpenML-CC18 datasets across six low-label budgets, LUCoS ranks first under mean AUC, ACC, and F1, with conclusions stable across metrics and dataset-level robustness checks. A gain decomposition reveals a simple mechanism: at the smallest budgets, the main benefit comes from enforcing coverage; as the budget increases, the decisive factor becomes the representation space in which coverage is measured. LUCoS mitigates failures of original feature space selection, showing that reliable unsupervised context selection depends less on selector sophistication than on defining representativeness in a meaningful representation geometry.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of selecting labeled instances in low-label tabular learning and introduces LUCoS, an unsupervised method that uses latent embedding geometry rather than raw tabular space for instance selection, significantly improving predictive performance.
AI.motivation: To improve labeled data selection in low-label tabular learning, especially in the cold-start setting, where no label information is initially available. This is key for enhancing predictive performance in tasks involving Tabular Foundation Models.
AI.method: The proposed method, LUCoS, selects representative medoids for labeled context based on the latent geometry induced by unsupervised embeddings from a Prior-Fitted Network (PFN), rather than relying on unreliable raw-feature distances.
AI.result: LUCoS outperformed other methods across 67 datasets in different low-label budgets, ranking first in metrics like AUC, ACC, and F1. Gains came from improved representativeness in the embedding space and stable performance across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised context selection for tabular data is more reliable when done in a meaningful, latent representation space rather than raw-feature space, showing strong performance with LUCoS in predicting across diverse datasets.

title: Kan Extension Transformers: A Categorical Unification of Attention, Diffusion, and Predict-Detach Self-Conditioning
summary: We propose Kan Extension Transformers (KETs) as a unifying categorical framework for a diverse group of Transformer implementations. The core claim is that a Transformer layer can be viewed as a weighted structured extension operator: standard attention is the singleton-neighborhood case, Geometric Transformer style incidence mixing is a sparse edge-restricted case, and KET is the higher-order simplicial case. This lens also clarifies a bridge to diffusion-style completion. When the extension operator acts on detached predictive carriers instead of teacher-forced hidden states, it becomes a valid self-conditioning mechanism that exposes noncausal structure without leaking gold future tokens. We include a comprehensive experimental validation of 12 different Transformer implementations varying across strict-causal and predict-detach regimes on Penn Treebank, WikiText-2, and WikiText-103. In the strict-causal setting, quadratic KET is the strongest model among the compared causal architectures on WikiText-2 and WikiText-103. Across all datasets, however, the largest gains come from the predict-detach regime rather than from changing the neighborhood family alone.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Kan Extension Transformers (KETs) as a categorical framework unifying various Transformer implementations by treating them as extension operators.
AI.motivation: To provide a foundational framework to better understand and unify diverse Transformer architectures and explore their connection to diffusion-style models.
AI.method: The authors reframe Transformer layers as weighted extension operators and propose a self-conditioning mechanism, "predict-detach," validated through experiments on three text datasets with several Transformer architectures.
AI.result: KET models show strong performance, particularly in the strict-causal setting on text datasets, with the most significant improvements observed in the predict-detach regime.
AI.conclusion: KETs offer a unified perspective and showcase their potential with strong empirical results while highlighting that structural changes in self-conditioning lead to the largest performance gains.

title: Garment Particles: A 2D--3D Symmetric Garment Representation for Generation and Editing
summary: Practical garment design spans two modes: intuitive creation from high-level intent, such as a reference image or text description, and complex low-level editing across 2D sewing patterns and 3D draped geometry, which requires professional training to navigate their complex interdependencies. Yet existing frameworks address only part of this challenge, offering either garment generation from casual inputs or direct editing on sewing patterns. To support both ends of the spectrum, we propose Garment Particles, a 5D point-cloud representation that jointly encodes 2D sewing patterns and 3D geometry. This representation enables Garment Particles Flow (GPF), a rectified flow framework that supports intuitive generation from high-level inputs (text, images, sketches) and various editing operations on 2D sewing patterns and 3D geometries via diffusion posterior sampling. Finally, we introduce Particles-to-Pattern Flow that converts generated garment particles into curved-based patterns for simulation. We validate our model's generation ability on multiple datasets, achieving state-of-the-art garment generation results against competitive baselines. Our model also enables many garment editing scenarios, including garment interpolation, sewing pattern editing, point-cloud- and silhouette-conditioned garment generation. Our project website is at https://garment-particles.github.io .

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Garment Particles, a new 5D representation for garment design that bridges high-level intent and low-level editing, enabling intuitive generation and comprehensive editing with state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing frameworks that focus on either high-level intent-based garment creation or low-level pattern editing, and to unify these processes into a comprehensive tool.
AI.method: The paper proposes a 5D point-cloud representation called Garment Particles and a rectified diffusion framework (Garment Particles Flow) for intuitive generation and editing. It includes a Particles-to-Pattern Flow module for converting designs into simulation-ready patterns.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in garment generation tasks across multiple datasets and supports various innovative garment editing features such as interpolation and conditioned generation.
AI.conclusion: Garment Particles successfully bridges the gap between intuitive design and professional editing, providing a versatile and powerful tool for practical garment creation.

title: Transfer Learning using 66 Diseases for Disease Forecasting Applications
summary: Disease forecasting models typically rely on a single data stream, making models brittle when histories are short or noisy. Recent top-performing models have shown that synthesizing multiple reporting systems for the same disease improves performance. Other recent work takes this idea a step further, using transfer learning to train a forecasting model for one disease using data from a different disease. We expand upon each of these approaches greatly, training machine learning models on data that span 66 infectious diseases and several data streams. We investigate the value of incorporating different data streams for forecasting 20 different disease data streams. We find that incorporating other data streams improves forecasting in the vast majority (84.9%) of time series and model structures considered. However, our work highlights that the quality of the added data matters, where adding data extremely different from the target data stream can sometimes degrade forecast performance. A major contribution of this work is in compiling a publicly-available database of data for use by the infectious disease forecasting community.

AI.tldr: The study explores the benefits of integrating data from various infectious disease streams to improve forecasting and highlights a database built for this purpose.
AI.motivation: To improve disease forecasting robustness by integrating diverse data streams and investigate the impact of incorporating heterogeneous data.
AI.method: Machine learning models were trained and analyzed on data covering 66 infectious diseases and multiple data streams while assessing the impact on 20 data streams.
AI.result: Incorporating additional data streams improved disease forecasting performance in 84.9% of cases, although lower-quality or irrelevant data could sometimes harm predictions.
AI.conclusion: Using diverse, high-quality data streams enhances forecasting performance, and the compiled database will support further developments in disease prediction.

title: MerLean-Prover: A Recursive Looping Harness for End-to-End Lean 4 Theorem Proving
summary: MerLean-Prover is an end-to-end Lean4 theorem prover that replaces sorry declarations with kernel-checkable proofs. It is built from three agent types (Planning, Check, and Lean) composed by a recursive outer loop whose unit of revision is the proof plan itself, and uses no fine-tuning, no custom RL objective, and no theorem-specific scaffolding. On FormalQualBench, a benchmark of 23 PhD-qualifying-exam theorems, MerLean-Prover solves 10/23, surpassing the strongest published open-source baseline (OpenGauss, 8/23). On Putnam2025, the same harness closes 12/12 with substantially lower total wall-clock than the next-best system that closes the full set. The harness also transfers to smaller models: Sonnet closes all four tested FormalQualBench problems, and Haiku closes the two short ones. These results suggest that harness design is a central factor in end-to-end Lean4 theorem proving, alongside raw model capability, and that a relatively simple harness can already be effective.

AI.tldr: MerLean-Prover is an end-to-end Lean4 theorem prover that improves theorem-solving performance with a simple, highly generalizable design.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in automating theorem proving, particularly aiming for efficient and kernel-checkable proofs without requiring fine-tuning or domain-specific customizations.
AI.method: The authors propose a system with three agents (Planning, Check, and Lean) forming a recursive process focused on proof plans, avoiding fine-tuning or custom RL objectives.
AI.result: The system solved 10/23 theorems on FormalQualBench, outperforming OpenGauss, and completed 12/12 on Putnam2025 faster than the alternatives. Smaller models also achieved high success rates.
AI.conclusion: Effective harness design is crucial in theorem proving, and relatively simple frameworks like MerLean-Prover can achieve strong performance without heavy customization.

title: Design First, Code Later: Aesthetically Pleasing Template-Free Slides Generation
summary: Producing presentation slides automatically entails coordinating narrative structure with page-level graphic design under strict spatial constraints. For such structured multimodal tasks, a well-organized design process is essential to ensure the final quality of slides. Existing approaches rely on fixed templates or directly emit executable code, thereby both limiting the creative layout-design capabilities of LLMs and bypassing the essential slide-page design step. To address these limitations, this paper (1) proposes a hierarchical slides generation workflow, DeepSlides, that systematically organizes slide design tasks without any predefined template or style, decoupling slide-page design from implementation; (2) introduces SlideDesign, a dataset tailored specifically for slides generation tasks; and (3) presents a multi-agent reinforcement learning training paradigm and trains a couple of models, SlideQwens, for slide design and implementation. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed framework outperforms baseline methods on evaluated metrics and achieves superior performance in human preference evaluations. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/sxswz213/DeepSlides.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DeepSlides, a framework for automated slide generation, addressing limitations of fixed templates by using a hierarchical workflow, a custom dataset, and multi-agent reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Current slide-generation approaches rely on fixed templates, limiting creativity and bypassing critical design steps. The authors seek to provide a more structured and flexible methodology.
AI.method: They propose the DeepSlides workflow, create the SlideDesign dataset, and use multi-agent reinforcement learning to train models for design and implementation tasks.
AI.result: Their framework outperforms baseline methods on various metrics and in human preference evaluations.
AI.conclusion: DeepSlides enhances slide generation by decoupling design and implementation, offering a more flexible and effective alternative to traditional template-based methods.

title: Falcon-X: A Time Series Foundation Model for Heterogeneous Multivariate Modeling
summary: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) are transforming the forecasting paradigm through large-scale cross-domain pretraining. However, most existing TSFMs remain univariate, and recent efforts to enable cross-variate modeling still operate directly within the raw variate space. This design introduces fundamental limitations in semantic alignment and relational expressivity. Specifically, raw-space group mixing lacks a dedicated mechanism to align heterogeneous physical quantities, while standard non-negative attention fails to capture the complex synergistic and antagonistic interactions ubiquitous in real-world systems. To address these challenges, we propose Falcon-X, decouples variates from the raw space and maps them into a unified latent prototype space. Falcon-X employs a Unified Prototype Diff-Attention mechanism that explicitly evaluates both positive and negative semantic affinities to explicitly align heterogeneous variates. Cross-variate interactions are then efficiently performed within this shared space via Latent Entity Attention, naturally facilitating zero-shot structural transfer. Finally, a Variate Reassembly Router robustly reconstructs variate-specific trajectories via a request-and-dispatch mechanism. Extensive evaluations on the GIFT-Eval and fev-bench benchmarks demonstrate that Falcon-X achieves state-of-the-art forecasting performance, offering a principled and scalable paradigm for complex multivariate environments. Falcon-X is publicly released to support future research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Falcon-X, a framework improving multivariate time series forecasting by mapping variables into a unified prototype space, enabling better semantic alignment and cross-variate interactions.
AI.motivation: Challenges exist in enabling effective cross-variate modeling in time series due to limitations in semantic alignment and interaction expressivity in raw-variable space.
AI.method: Falcon-X introduces a decoupling mechanism that maps variables into a latent prototype space, implements a Unified Prototype Diff-Attention mechanism for affinity evaluation, and reconstructs trajectories via a Variate Reassembly Router.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations demonstrate Falcon-X achieves state-of-the-art forecasting performance in benchmarks GIFT-Eval and fev-bench.
AI.conclusion: Falcon-X provides a scalable and principled approach to multivariate forecasting and facilitates advances in handling heterogeneous and complex interactions.

title: BAIT: Boundary-Guided Disclosure Escalation via Self-Conditioned Reasoning
summary: In this work, we propose BAIT (Boundary-Aware Iterative Trap), a three-step jailbreak framework that approaches malicious goals through internal disclosure. BAIT first asks the model to identify the protection boundary, then requires it to refine that boundary, and finally requests a detailed example. By expanding each step upon the model's previous responses, BAIT turns the model's own reasoning and consistency tendency into a disclosure pathway. Experiments on AdvBench, JailbreakBench, AIR-Bench, and SORRY-Bench demonstrate that BAIT consistently achieves strong attack success rates across top-tier large language models, significantly advancing conventional jailbreak baselines. Further analysis reveals that: 1) prevention-oriented framing significantly outperforms direct knowledge request; 2) the refinement step plays a critical role in disclosure escalation; and 3) the first two steps have a certain chance of eliciting harmful content while triggering little filtering.

AI.tldr: BAIT is a three-step jailbreak framework to exploit language model protections by iteratively refining and crossing protection boundaries, achieving high success rates in experimental benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To explore new methods of circumventing safeguards in large language models by leveraging their own reasoning and consistency tendencies.
AI.method: BAIT consists of three iterative steps: identifying the model's protection boundary, refining that boundary, and eliciting detailed examples based on prior responses.
AI.result: BAIT outperformed traditional jailbreak methods in attack success rates in experiments conducted on several benchmarks like AdvBench and JailbreakBench.
AI.conclusion: BAIT is an effective strategy for bypassing LLM safeguards by exploiting their reasoning patterns, with key steps such as refinement and prevention-oriented framing playing crucial roles.

title: Normal Guidance is what Attention Needs
summary: We consider training classifiers for 3D medical images using only one binary label for the entire volume rather than a label for each 2D slice. In such weakly supervised settings, can we learn accurate classifiers for slice-level predictions? Attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) can produce an attention score for every slice. Yet recent work demonstrates that a simple center-focused baseline that ignores image content can outperform attention-based and transformer-based MIL at slice-level classification of 3D brain scans. We show this baseline also outperforms existing MIL at slice-level classification of thoracic and abdominal CT scans. Motivated by this baseline, we propose Normal Guidance, a regularization technique that encourages the learned attention distribution to follow a bell-shaped curve. Across three medical imaging datasets totaling over 4 million 2D slices, we show our Normal Guidance enables attention-based and transformer-based MIL methods to deliver significantly better slice-level localization than the state-of-the-art while remaining competitive at whole-scan classification.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenge of training classifiers for 3D medical images using weak supervision and proposes a novel regularization technique, Normal Guidance, to improve slice-level localization.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of current weakly supervised methods that struggle with accurate slice-level classification in 3D medical imaging.
AI.method: The authors propose Normal Guidance, a regularization method that guides attention distributions to follow a bell-shaped curve in multiple instance learning (MIL) settings.
AI.result: Normal Guidance outperforms existing MIL approaches at slice-level localization across three datasets totaling over 4 million slices while remaining competitive on whole-scan classifications.
AI.conclusion: Normal Guidance enhances weakly supervised slice-level classification by enabling attention-based and transformer-based MIL methods to achieve state-of-the-art results in localization tasks.

title: Measuring Prediction Uncertainty in Neural Cellular Automata
comment: Accepted for publication at the 29th International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention - MICCAI 2026
summary: Neural cellular automata (NCA) provide a lightweight alternative to encoder-decoder segmentation networks. However, it can be difficult to decide when a prediction should be trusted. Here, we study uncertainty estimation for NCA-based medical image segmentation without modifying the underlying architecture or retraining the model. Our approach is motivated by viewing the NCA as a dynamical system where convergent attractors correspond to confident predictions. Concretely, we propose resilience, a simple measure that leverages the intrinsic iterative structure of NCAs by probing the stability of the final prediction under small perturbations of the automaton state. Predictions that return to the same solution are deemed confident, while those that change substantially are flagged as uncertain. We evaluate uncertainty by its ability to predict segmentation quality using selective prediction metrics ($Δ$Dice@90 and AURC) and ranking metrics (AUROC and AUPRC). Across multiple medical segmentation benchmarks, resilience identifies failure cases more reliably than baselines, improving trust and safety in NCA-based models.

AI.tldr: Neural Cellular Automata (NCA) are explored for medical image segmentation, focusing on uncertainty estimation without retraining the model. A measure called resilience identifies confident predictions through stability against perturbations.
AI.motivation: To address uncertainty in medical image segmentation using NCAs, ensuring trust in predictions without altering the model architecture or requiring retraining.
AI.method: The NCA is treated as a dynamical system. Resilience is proposed as an uncertainty metric, evaluating prediction stability under perturbations. Metrics like $Δ$Dice@90, AUROC, AURC, and AUPRC are used.
AI.result: Resilience outperformed baseline methods across multiple benchmarks by reliably identifying failure cases and predicting segmentation quality.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates resilience as an effective tool for enhancing trust and safety in NCA-based medical image segmentation by identifying uncertainty without model modification.

title: Greening AI Inference with Accuracy and Latency-aware User Incentives
summary: The widespread use of AI services has raised concerns for its environmental sustainability, towards which recent studies have identified carbon emissions of AI inference as the major contributor. This paper introduces a framework for designing AI inference incentives based on the users' valuation for inference quality and latency, together with their environmental consciousness, while accounting for the tradeoff between carbon emissions and the two QoE parameters. Our approach can accommodate different tradeoffs, that depend on the size and complexity of the AI models and the allocation of resources to serve inference requests. The incentives can be offered through a practical two-tier service subscription that offers users a discount in exchange for reduced carbon emissions. The discounted service option gives the AI provider the flexibility to serve some percentage of inference requests at a lower quality and higher latency during periods of high carbon intensity.

AI.tldr: The paper designs a framework for incentivizing eco-friendly AI inference use, by balancing quality, latency, and carbon footprint through a two-tier subscription model.
AI.motivation: Growing concerns regarding the environmental impact of AI services, specifically carbon emissions from AI inference, drive the need to create sustainable models for AI deployment.
AI.method: A framework is introduced to design incentives based on user valuation of inference quality, latency, and their environmental consciousness. It incorporates tradeoffs based on model complexity and resource allocation.
AI.result: The study proposes a practical two-tier subscription model, offering discounts for users willing to accept lower performance during high carbon intensity periods, thus enabling resource flexibility.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that incentivizing environmentally conscious behavior through service tiering can effectively balance user experience and carbon reduction in AI services.

title: Guiding LLM Post-training Data Engineering with Model Internals from Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Model internals encode rich information about how a large language model (LLM) processes its training data; however, post-training data engineering largely relies on external signals and ignores rich intrinsic signals lying in model internals. We propose SAERL, a data engineering framework for LLM reinforcement learning (RL). It models three intrinsic data properties: diversity, difficulty, and quality, using model internals extracted with Sparse Autoencoder (SAE), an advanced mechanistic interpretability tool. Each property grounds a concrete data engineering operation: SAE-space clustering with moderate batch mixing for batch diversity control, a difficulty proxy for easy-to-hard curriculum ordering, and a quality probe for data filtering. SAERL improves average accuracy by 3.00% over vanilla GRPO and reaches target accuracy with 20% fewer training steps on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, with consistent gains across model scales and RL algorithms. Experiments show that SAE transfers effectively across model families and scales, serving as a lightweight and reusable data engineering tool. These results demonstrate that model internals are a powerful and practical source of signals for post-training data engineering.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAERL, a data engineering framework for LLM reinforcement learning, leveraging intrinsic signals from model internals to improve training efficiency and accuracy.
AI.motivation: To leverage intrinsic signals from large language models' internals, rather than relying solely on external signals, for more effective post-training data engineering.
AI.method: The authors use Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to model three data properties—diversity, difficulty, quality—for data engineering. They perform clustering for diversity control, employ curriculum ordering for difficulty, and use quality probes for data filtering.
AI.result: SAERL improves average model accuracy by 3.00% and reduces training steps required to reach target accuracy by 20%, with consistent benefits across model scales and reinforcement learning algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Model internals can serve as reliable signals for data engineering, making SAERL a practical and reusable framework for enhancing LLM reinforcement learning outcomes.

title: Probabilistic Smoothing with Ratio-Monotone Transforms for Global Optimization
summary: Probabilistic smoothing is a standard tool for global optimization, but existing methods rely on Gaussian kernels and specific transforms, often resulting in strong hyperparameter sensitivity and limited robustness. We propose a general smoothing framework that combines flexible symmetric unimodal kernels with monotonic ratio-based transformations. Under mild conditions, we show that the smoothed objective preserves the global maximizer and that all stationary points concentrate near the true optimum for sufficiently large amplification, without requiring a decreasing smoothing schedule. We further provide explicit complexity bounds for stochastic gradient ascent and show that a leave-one-out baseline provably reduces variance. Experiments on high-dimensional benchmarks and black-box adversarial attacks demonstrate improved robustness and competitive performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new probabilistic smoothing framework for global optimization that uses flexible kernels and transformations, showing improvements in robustness and performance compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing probabilistic smoothing techniques suffer from hyperparameter sensitivity and limited robustness due to reliance on Gaussian kernels and specific transforms.
AI.method: The framework uses symmetric unimodal kernels with monotonic ratio-based transformations, ensuring preserved global maximizers and concentrated stationary points near the true optimum without requiring a decreasing smoothing schedule.
AI.result: The approach provides explicit complexity bounds for stochastic gradient ascent and demonstrates improved robustness and performance in high-dimensional benchmarks and black-box adversarial attacks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enhances the robustness and efficacy of probabilistic smoothing for optimization tasks, offering a theoretically backed and empirically validated method.

title: MobileMoE: Scaling On-Device Mixture of Experts
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has become the de facto architecture for hundred-billion-parameter language models, yet its advantages at sub-billion scales for on-device deployment remain largely unexplored. To close this gap, we present MobileMoE, a family of on-device MoE language models with sub-billion active parameters (0.3-0.9B active and 1.3-5.3B total) that establish a new Pareto frontier for on-device LLMs. We first formulate an on-device MoE scaling law that jointly optimizes MoE architecture under mobile memory and compute constraints, identifying an on-device sweet spot - moderate sparsity with fine-grained and shared experts - that is simultaneously memory and compute-optimal. Building on the derived architectures, we train MobileMoE with a four-stage recipe covering pre-training, mid-training, instruction fine-tuning, and quantization-aware training, all on open-source datasets. Across 14 benchmarks, MobileMoE matches or exceeds leading on-device dense LLMs with 2-4$\times$ fewer inference FLOPs, and matches or surpasses the state-of-the-art MoE OLMoE-1B-7B with up to 60% fewer parameters. To bridge the last mile to mobile deployment, we provide the first efficient MoE inference on commodity smartphones with comprehensive on-device profiling. At comparable INT4 weight memory, MobileMoE-S delivers $1.8$-$3.8\times$ faster prefill and $2.2$-$3.4\times$ faster decode than the dense baseline MobileLLM-Pro.

AI.tldr: MobileMoE introduces sub-billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models for on-device LLMs, achieving state-of-the-art efficiency and performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: While MoE architectures dominate in large-scale language models, their potential and optimization for smaller scales suitable for mobile and on-device applications have not been thoroughly explored.
AI.method: The authors developed MobileMoE, optimizing MoE scaling laws for memory and compute constraints. They used a 4-stage training process including pre-training, mid-training, fine-tuning, and quantization, and ensured efficient deployment on smartphones.
AI.result: MobileMoE outperformed dense LLM baselines in benchmarks with up to 4× fewer FLOPs. It also surpassed state-of-the-art MoE models with up to 60% fewer parameters and showed significant speed gains during inference on mobile devices.
AI.conclusion: MobileMoE establishes a new standard for on-device LLM efficiency and performance, demonstrating the feasibility and benefits of optimized MoE designs for mobile applications.

title: Do Modern Post-Hoc Watermarking Methods Beat Broken-Arrows?
